text: --- ## Top Stories ### The US Military Buildup Against Iran: Largest Since 2003 The Trump administration has assembled what military analysts are now describing as the most concentrated display of US military force in the Middle East since the 2003 invasion of Iraq. Four American carrier strike groups are either in the region or converging on it: the USS Gerald R. Ford arrived at Souda Bay on the island of Crete on Monday and is expected off the coast of Israel within days; the USS Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group is operating in the Arabian Sea. More than 40,000 US military personnel are now stationed across bases and naval assets in the region. Robert Pape, professor of political science at the University of Chicago and a leading analyst of American air power, wrote Saturday: "This represents 40-50% of the deployable US air power in the world. Think air power on the order of the 1991 and 2003 Iraq war. And growing. Never has the US deployed this much force against a potential enemy and not launched strikes." Col. Richard Kemp, former commander of British forces in Afghanistan, told the Media Line that the current scale already exceeds the June 2025 "12-day war" (Operation Midnight Hammer) and "could run into weeks." The State Department on Monday ordered the evacuation of non-essential personnel and family members from the US Embassy in Beirut — 32 to 50 staff and family members flew out of Beirut airport. The State Department updated its travel advisory for Lebanon, warning US citizens not to travel to the country. The WSWS's Andre Damon noted that this evacuation follows the identical pattern established before Operation Midnight Hammer in June 2025, when US presence was similarly reduced at embassies in Baghdad, Kuwait, and Bahrain in the days before B-2 bombers struck Iran's nuclear facilities. The Washington Post reported separately that the Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman, Gen. Dan Caine, has privately warned the president that a US attack on Iran carries "acute risks" — including a lack of munitions and allied support — that differ sharply from Trump's public boasting that Caine had told him an attack would be an "easy victory." This contradiction between Trump's public claims and military reality is itself a dangerous indicator: it suggests the president is building a public justification for war that has been deliberately detached from the military's own risk assessments. From the WSWS's perspective, the bourgeois media's framing — the Financial Times headline "Crisis of his own making" is typical — obscures the bipartisan character of the war drive. The Democrats' objections to Trump on Iran are tactical, not principled. The NYT analysis noting that Tehran views concessions on uranium enrichment as riskier than war should be understood not as Iranian intransigence but as the rational response of a state that has watched how the United States treated Libya and Iraq after they gave up their weapons programs. The fourth anniversary of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine (February 24, 2022) falls today. Four years in, the UN reports more than 15,000 civilians killed, including 763 children. Russia holds approximately 20 percent of Ukrainian territory, and front lines are largely frozen. Ukraine's Zelenskyy told the FT that the war is at "the beginning of the end" — but what that end looks like remains contested. Negotiations are now under way, with the NYT reporting that a major sticking point is the fate of Ukrainian citizens in Russian-controlled Donetsk, who face an "agonizing choice" about their political future. Hungary is blocking both the latest EU sanctions package on Russia and a $106 billion financial aid package to Kyiv, a pressure point that significantly weakens the Western position going into any talks. The BBC published a striking report today: four Russian soldiers testify that they witnessed fellow troops executed by commanders for refusing orders. This is not simply a human interest story. It is evidence of the coercive, non-voluntary character of Russia's own war effort — a point that connects to the WSWS's analysis of the war as driven by competing ruling class interests, with the working class on both sides bearing the catastrophic costs. ### Trump's State of the Union: Reeling from Supreme Court, Falling Polls Tonight's State of the Union address is being delivered under conditions the White House itself acknowledges are difficult. The NYT reports Trump is "bruised and unpopular," reeling from the 6-3 Supreme Court decision last Friday invalidating his "reciprocal tariffs" under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). His approval ratings are declining. The paper notes that "cracks appear in Trump coalition" in bellwether regions like Northeastern Pennsylvania, where working-class discontent is growing. The circumstances around tonight's speech are significant beyond the speech itself. The WSWS Perspectives published today — "The real state of Trump's America" — provides the counter-narrative that the speech will attempt to bury. Key facts that Trump will not mention: the national debt stands at $38.4 trillion ($113,000 per person), growing at $8 billion per day; cumulative price increases since 2020 include food up 25%, housing still climbing 3% annually; the top 1% control 31.7% of all wealth — the highest share since the Fed began tracking in 1989; Elon Musk's fortune has ballooned to $726 billion; CEO pay is 285 times average worker pay; 2025 was the deadliest year for ICE detention on record, with 66,000 people currently held. The Democrats have selected Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger — a CIA veteran — to deliver the party's response, just as they chose Senator Elissa Slotkin (also a CIA veteran) last year. The WSWS Perspectives correctly identifies this as a deliberate signal to the ruling class that the Democratic Party will uphold the military-intelligence apparatus regardless of the electoral outcome. Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez will offer their customary "left"-sounding rhetoric; in practice, the Democrats collaborate with Trump on the core class interests of the financial oligarchy. The "Angel Families" staging at the White House on Monday — families of people killed by undocumented immigrants — is the latest in Trump's cynical exploitation of victims to normalize the immigration crackdown. The NYT notes that public support for Trump's immigration approach is "softening." Hanging over the proceedings is the Jeffrey Epstein scandal. The WSWS Editorial Board's Perspectives makes this explicit: "The Justice Department's release of nearly 3 million pages has shaken the upper tiers of corporate America." Billionaire Leslie Wexner was identified as a co-conspirator in 2019 FBI documents yet faces no charges. The arrest in London of former UK Ambassador to the US Peter Mandelson on suspicion of "misconduct in public office" — tied to Epstein revelations — is a separate but connected story that reveals how far the rot extends into the transatlantic ruling class. ### Supreme Court Strikes Down Trump Tariffs — Trump Escalates Immediately Last Friday's 6-3 Supreme Court ruling in Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump struck down the "reciprocal tariffs" Trump had imposed under the IEEPA — a ruling that voided approximately $160 billion in tariffs collected over the past year. The decision was written by Chief Justice John Roberts, with Justices Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett joining the three liberals in the majority. Trump's response was immediate and revealing. At a hastily convened press conference, he attacked the three conservative justices who voted against him as "fools and lapdogs of the radical left," called the entire majority "major sleazebags" and "foreign country-centric," and compared himself to a Mafia don warning foreign countries that they would stop "dancing in the streets." The Wall Street Journal editorialized that "Trump owes the Supreme Court an apology." But the more significant story is what Trump did immediately after the ruling. He invoked Section 122 of the 1974 Trade Act — a provision never previously used at this scale by any president — to impose a 10-15% tariff on all imported goods for 150 days, after which Congressional approval is required for extension. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent described this as temporary. In the meantime, Trump has directed his legal team to pursue longer-term tariff authority under Section 301 of the same act. The Guardian is covering a related development: the 10% tariffs that came into effect Tuesday — the "replacement" tariffs following the Supreme Court ruling — are being greeted with anger by trading partners globally. China has simultaneously imposed export restrictions on 20 Japanese companies with defense ties, escalating the US-China confrontation in a different theater. The WSWS Perspectives by John Burton correctly identifies the Supreme Court ruling not as a restoration of constitutional norms but as an expression of deepening divisions within the ruling class over the strategic direction of US capitalism. Trump's willingness to attack three of his own appointees exposes how far the presidency has moved from any conventional understanding of institutional loyalty. ### Mexico: El Mencho Killed — US Intelligence Decisive — Violence Erupts On Sunday, Mexican security forces, with decisive assistance from US Northern Command intelligence, killed Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes — "El Mencho" — leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG), in a compound in Tapalpa, Jalisco. El Mencho was tracked after surveillance of a romantic partner he traveled to visit. Defense Secretary Ricardo Trevilla acknowledged that "the exchange of information and data" with US Northern Command was "how we arrived at this specific case." The operation killed 25 soldiers, 30 alleged CJNG members, and 3 civilians, including a pregnant woman. 70 arrests were made across seven states. The Mexican government immediately deployed 10,000 additional troops nationwide. In the days since, violence has erupted across western Mexico: schools shut in several states, businesses closed, US citizens were warned to shelter in place in Puerto Vallarta and Cancún. Guadalajara — a 2026 World Cup host city — descended into chaos. The WSWS's Andrea Lobo provides the essential class analysis: the "war on drugs" is not a humanitarian project but a mechanism for deepening US imperialist control over Mexico and justifying the militarization of the country's internal security apparatus — directed ultimately against the Mexican working class. The killing of El Mencho does not end the cartel; it will likely trigger a violent succession struggle within CJNG and provoke inter-cartel warfare, which will in turn be used as a pretext for expanded military deployment. The broader context is the Trump administration's ongoing coercion of the Mexican government. Trump has continued to demand that Mexico "step up" its anti-cartel efforts even after this major operation, signaling that no level of compliance will satisfy Washington's demands. The CIA's role (reported by WaPo) in tracking El Mencho further underscores that this is a US intelligence operation conducted on Mexican soil with the acquiescence of a government under severe imperial pressure. ### Kaiser Strike and NewYork-Presbyterian Nurses: Twin Betrayals in One Day Two major healthcare worker strikes were brought to anticlimactic ends within the past 48 hours, exposing the systematic function of the union bureaucracy as a mechanism for demobilizing workers rather than representing them. **Kaiser Permanente:** On Monday, UNAC/UHCP officials ordered an abrupt end to the four-week strike of 31,000 healthcare workers — nurses, pharmacists, and clinicians in California and Hawaii. Workers were told to return by 7:00 AM Tuesday. No contract was ratified. No membership vote was held. The union described "significant movement at the bargaining table" without disclosing any specifics. By calling the action "suspended" rather than ending it through ratification, the leadership bypassed democratic procedures entirely and used its own discretionary authority to demobilize the strike. The WSWS's Marc Wells correctly identifies this as "a flagrant violation of members' basic democratic rights." **NewYork-Presbyterian:** On Saturday, NYSNA officials announced the ratification of a contract ending a six-week strike by 4,000 nurses — the last of four facilities still on strike. The key facts: nurses had overwhelmingly rejected the previous tentative agreement just over a week before; the new ratification vote was conducted hours after the deal was announced, with no time for workers to read or discuss the contract; the vote was conducted online under conditions that NYSNA itself had already acknowledged as insecure (the previous vote was on SurveyMonkey); and the union had isolated nurses in below-freezing temperatures while withholding strike pay to coerce ratification. The contract does not adequately address safe staffing. These two betrayals on the same day, combined with the WGA West staff strike — in which 115 administrative workers are striking against their own union — reveal the trade union apparatus as an instrument of class collaboration, not class struggle. The developing strike wave is real; the question is whether workers can build the rank-and-file organizations necessary to wage it independently of the bureaucracy. --- ## International Developments ### Ukraine War: Year Five — Stalemate, Atrocities, and Deteriorating Western Position The fourth anniversary of Russia's February 24, 2022 full-scale invasion brings no resolution. The NYT reports that Ukraine faces an "agonizing choice" over the fate of residents in Russian-controlled Donetsk — people who have lived under occupation and may face a peace deal that leaves them formally under Russian sovereignty. The BBC published testimony from four Russian soldiers claiming they witnessed their own comrades executed for refusing to advance. Russia's war economy is increasingly strained. A major NYT analysis reveals that approximately half of Russia's federal budget now goes to the Ukraine war — spending that "does little to support its long-term development" while enriching defense contractors and a narrow military-industrial stratum. The ruble has been partially stabilized through capital controls and commodity exports, but Russia's long-term productive capacity is being hollowed out. Meanwhile, the Western coalition supporting Ukraine is fracturing. Hungary — under the Orban government — is blocking both the latest EU sanctions package against Russia and a €96 billion ($106 billion) financial aid package to Kyiv, according to the NYT. This gives Russia a diplomatic opening and significantly weakens the position of the NATO bloc going into any ceasefire talks. The EU's ability to sustain Ukraine economically is a political, not just a financial, question. Ukraine's President Zelenskyy told the Financial Times that the war is "at the beginning of the end" — a formulation that is diplomatically significant because it signals Kyiv's openness to negotiations while trying to manage expectations. The actual negotiating positions remain far apart: Russia's demands for territorial concessions and Ukrainian neutrality remain unacceptable to Kyiv, and the social base for a compromise peace in Ukraine itself is narrow, as the NYT's reporting from Donetsk makes clear. ### France: Fascist Mobilization and the Macron Government's Complicity The death of Quentin Deranque — a young supporter of the far-right organization Lyon Populaire who died following a confrontation after a lecture by France Unbowed's Palestinian-origin Euro-MP Rima Hassan — has been seized upon by the entire French political establishment to launch a coordinated offensive against the left. The WSWS's Anthony Torres identifies the key political fact: a political front spanning fascist groups, the Socialist Party, the National Rally (Marine Le Pen and Jordan Bardella), and President Macron's own government has formed around the Deranque death to criminalize left-wing political activity. Macron explicitly blessed the far-right demonstration in Deranque's honor at the opening of the Agricultural Fair. The French government has refused to ban the fascist mobilization while simultaneously using the event to attack France Unbowed and the broader left. The WSWS draws the parallel with the United States, where the death of fascist agitator Charlie Kirk was similarly exploited to mobilize the right and delegitimize opposition. The objective in France, as Torres writes, is to clear the way for either Le Pen or Bardella to assume power after the forthcoming local elections and 2027 presidential election. The US ambassador to France, Charles Kushner (son of Jared Kushner's father), further inflamed the situation by citing "violent radical leftism" in the Deranque death — a formulation the French government found unacceptable and formally protested, summoning Kushner twice. He declined to appear on both occasions, sending a junior official in his place. ### Australia: Democratic Rights Under Labor Attack on Multiple Fronts The Albanese Labor government's assault on democratic rights is accelerating on several fronts simultaneously, all of which the WSWS's Australian correspondents are documenting: **The Bondi terrorist attack cover-up:** More than two months after the December 14 attack in which IS-linked father-son pair Sajid and Naveed Akram killed 15 people at a Jewish gathering at Bondi Beach, Oscar Grenfell reports that the central political question — how known-to-ASIO perpetrators were able to plan, arm, and execute the attack without interception — remains entirely unanswered. The attack is being invoked to justify sweeping new powers (protest bans, political party proscription) without any scrutiny of the intelligence and security failures that allowed it to occur. **Banning Hizb-ut Tahrir:** Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke has initiated the banning of Hizb-ut Tahrir under new legislation that does not require any terrorism link — only political speech deemed to promote "hate speech." Burke explicitly boasted that this is "the first time we have been able to ban a group which falls short of a terrorist listing." The legislation, as the WSWS has previously reported, is structured in a way that could be used to ban political parties. **Police raid on anti-fascist artwork:** On February 18, three ACT Policing officers entered the Dissent Cafe and Bar in Canberra after an anonymous Crime Stoppers complaint about satirical posters depicting Trump, Musk, Netanyahu, Vance, and Putin in Nazi-style uniforms, produced by British protest collective Grow Up Art. When the owner refused to surrender the posters, police declared the premises a crime scene for more than two hours. Detectives from the Major Crimes Unit were later dispatched to seize the posters. The action is being treated as a test case under the Combatting Antisemitism, Hate and Extremism Act 2026. **Healthcare and education strikes betrayed:** The ANMFSA (South Australian nurses' union) suspended all industrial action after a backroom deal with the Labor state government that fell far short of workers' 21% pay demand. In Victoria, early childhood educators are striking Thursday — but the AEU is keeping them separated from the March 24 school teacher stoppage to prevent unified action against the Labor government. ### Israel-Palestine: Settlers, Annexation, and the Berlinale Protests Twenty countries issued a joint statement condemning Israel's "de facto annexation" drive in the occupied West Bank, calling it a "deliberate and direct attack on the viability of a Palestinian state." The statement represents a significant diplomatic development but carries no enforcement mechanism. The NYT published a detailed report by Azam Ahmed on the systematic dispossession of Palestinian families in the West Bank through settler violence. The Rezeq Abu Naim family, after two years of continuous attacks on their land at all hours and in all forms, finally abandoned their home after another violent incursion over the weekend. This is not exceptional; it is the operational method of what twenty countries are now officially calling annexation. At the Berlin International Film Festival (Berlinale), award-winning filmmakers used the closing ceremony to denounce German government complicity in Gaza genocide. Stefan Steinberg's WSWS report describes how festival management — including director Tricia Tuttle and jury head Wim Wenders — deliberately sought to prevent any political discussion of Palestine, with Wenders initially declaring artists should "stay out of politics." The protest from filmmakers was so sustained and forceful that it dominated the closing ceremony despite these attempts at suppression. The political character of the German cultural establishment's silence on genocide is itself a story. ### UK: Mandelson Arrest, Epstein Fallout, and the Crisis of Starmerism The arrest on Monday of Peter Mandelson — former UK Ambassador to the United States, key architect of Blairite "New Labour," and longtime central figure in the Labour establishment — on suspicion of "misconduct in public office" has sent shockwaves through British politics. The arrest followed revelations about Mandelson's dealings with Jeffrey Epstein. This is the deepest penetration of the Epstein scandal into the British political establishment since the arrest of Prince Andrew earlier this month. The NYT reports that the Mandelson arrest "deepens a scandal that has led to calls for Prime Minister Keir Starmer's resignation." Starmer's Labour government, which came to power in 2024 promising a break from the corruption of the Conservative years, is now implicated through its most senior figures in the Epstein network's exposure. The political implications extend beyond Labour's domestic difficulties. Mandelson is a figure who has operated at the intersection of transatlantic ruling class networks for decades. His arrest, combined with the UK's move to back Australia on removing Prince Andrew from the line of succession, suggests that the Epstein exposure is beginning to consume figures previously considered untouchable. ### China-Japan Economic Confrontation Escalates China placed export restrictions on 20 Japanese companies with defense industry ties — the latest escalation in an ongoing economic confrontation between Beijing and Tokyo that has developed alongside Japan's rearmament drive. The NYT frames this as a "monthslong feud." The broader context is China's role in the global economic restructuring driven by the end of the post-Cold War liberal order: Beijing is now explicitly using export controls on critical materials (rare earths, semiconductor components) as a geopolitical weapon in the same way the US uses financial sanctions. The WSWS has not yet covered this specific development. It connects directly to: the Taiwan semiconductor scenario (NYT published a major analysis today of the vulnerability of US tech industry if China cuts off Taiwan chip exports); the broader US-China military confrontation; and the Japanese government's decision to dramatically increase defense spending under US pressure. --- ## United States ### Georgia Concentration Camp: 10,000-Bed Detention Complex The Trump administration's plans for a 10,000-bed immigrant detention complex in Social Circle, Georgia — detailed in Jacob Crosse's WSWS report — represent the largest planned civilian mass detention facility since the Japanese American internment of World War II. Utility filings project more than one million gallons per day in wastewater demand, consistent with permanent large-scale operation. The broader development plan encompasses more than 2.3 million square feet. This is not a "surge" measure. It is infrastructure for indefinite mass incarceration on a scale that would make the United States the world's leading incarcerator of a targeted ethnic and political group. The plan is provoking outrage — which the bourgeois press covers — but without the class analysis: this concentration camp network is not simply an immigration enforcement tool. It is, as the WSWS Perspectives notes, the infrastructure of a police state directed ultimately against the entire working class, beginning with its most vulnerable sector. Congress has already allocated $45 billion for detention expansion, with projected capacity of 135,000 — double the current record level of 66,000. ### Quakertown, Pennsylvania: Students Jailed, Police Cover-Up At least five high school students from Quakertown Community High School in Bucks County, Pennsylvania remain in police custody more than 72 hours after being arrested during an anti-ICE walkout on Friday, February 20. The incident began when Police Chief Scott McElree — not in uniform — physically grabbed students during the protest, with video widely circulated showing him choking a young female student. Another officer was filmed throwing a student into a planter. Five students and one adult were arrested. As of this writing, no charging documents have been released, no body camera footage has been shared, and the department has issued no public statement explaining McElree's use of force. A Change.org petition demanding McElree's resignation has exceeded 7,800 signatures; a GoFundMe for legal and medical costs has raised nearly $33,000. The IYSSE statement published today correctly frames this not as an isolated incident of police misconduct but as part of a "broader campaign to intimidate youth, criminalize political opposition and normalize police-state methods." Student walkouts against ICE have now occurred in more than 36 states. Will Lehman, the Mack Trucks worker and UAW presidential candidate, issued a statement calling the Quakertown assault "police brutality against children" and demanding working class solidarity with the students. The 323-student suspension at Woodbridge Senior High School in Prince William County, Virginia — for participating in an anti-ICE walkout — is documented in the IYSSE statement. Administrative punishment is being used alongside police violence as a two-pronged strategy to suppress the youth movement. ### Pentagon, Iran, and the Military-Industrial Budget The Pentagon is racing to spend $153 billion in added funds — money Republicans pushed through in their "sweeping domestic policy bill" — while having ignored Congress's demands for a spending plan for months. This is the militarization of the US economy in real time, directed toward war preparations rather than social needs. The Anthropic-Pentagon confrontation is a significant development: the Defense Department summoned Anthropic's CEO after the AI company demanded guardrails be maintained in a major defense contract. This reveals how AI is being conscripted into the military apparatus, and the pressure being placed on tech companies to remove safety restrictions for military applications. The Pentagon separately pulled its support for an air safety bill — legislation written in response to the January 2025 D.C. helicopter-plane collision — just as the House was expected to vote on it. This means the safety improvements prompted by a disaster that killed dozens of people are being blocked by the military. ### The Epstein Files and the Decomposition of the American Ruling Class The WSWS Perspectives today weaves the Epstein scandal into its analysis of the State of the Union — and rightly so. The Justice Department's release of nearly 3 million pages has now resulted in: Attorney General Pam Bondi deflecting questions by citing the Dow Jones index; FBI documents showing billionaire Leslie Wexner identified as a co-conspirator in 2019 with no charges brought; and email evidence of Epstein's own clinical dehumanization of the girls he trafficked. Trump himself is deeply implicated. The British dimension — Mandelson's arrest, Andrew's earlier arrest — reveals that this is not an American aberration but a feature of the transatlantic ruling class as a social formation. David North's characterization, cited in the Perspectives, of the Epstein revelations as revealing "the social physiognomy of a degenerate ruling class and oligarchical society in an advanced state of decomposition" is the correct political-historical framework for understanding what the files expose: not the crimes of bad individuals, but the systematic character of a social class that has detached itself from any restraining legal or moral framework. --- ## Science and Public Health ### Bhattacharya Controls NIH and CDC — Measles Surges — Public Health Infrastructure Being Demolished The Trump administration confirmed last week that NIH Director Jay Bhattacharya — best known as the Stanford health economist who co-authored the Great Barrington Declaration advocating for COVID-19 "herd immunity" — will simultaneously serve as acting director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Benjamin Mateus's WSWS analysis is essential reading. Bhattacharya has no formal training in public health administration and no experience directing infectious disease response. His "primary qualification," as Mateus correctly writes, "stems from his role during the COVID-19 pandemic as a leading propagandist for the mass infection policies demanded by Wall Street." The Great Barrington Declaration was written at the American Institute for Economic Research, a libertarian think tank tied to Charles Koch, and advocated "herd immunity" — a position that the WSWS's Global Workers' Inquest into the COVID-19 Pandemic has extensively documented as a deliberate policy of mass death in the service of capitalist profit. Now the same person controls both the NIH — the US government's primary biomedical research agency — and the CDC — its leading disease surveillance institution. This is occurring while measles cases are surging across the United States: a preventable disease returning in the context of RFK Jr.'s anti-vaccination campaign (Kennedy last week defended Trump's order ramping up glyphosate production before angry "MAHA Moms" who had not anticipated he would take that position on pesticides). The dismantling of public health is not a policy failure; it is a policy success from the standpoint of the financial interests who want to remove regulatory obstacles to profit. The Supreme Court's agreement to consider whether states can sue fossil fuel companies over greenhouse gas emissions is a separate development but part of the same pattern: the systematic weakening of regulatory constraints on capital. Dozens of municipalities are seeking billions in damages; a positive ruling for plaintiffs could have significant financial implications for the oil and gas industry. --- ## WSWS Coverage — Last 24 Hours ### "The real state of Trump's America: Social misery, dictatorship, war — and an upsurge of class struggle" — by WSWS Editorial Board **[FULL TEXT — Perspectives article published February 24, 2026]** US President Donald Trump will deliver the annual State of the Union address Tuesday night before a joint session of Congress. Were it honestly titled, the speech would be called "The State of the Rich"—a demagogic exercise in self-congratulation, burying the catastrophic reality of American life beneath a mountain of lies. The speech is being delivered in the year that the United States is to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. On July 4, 2026, the nation will commemorate the issuing of the document that proclaimed "all men are created equal" and asserted the inalienable rights of "Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness." The founders fought a revolution against King George III. Two hundred and fifty years later, Trump's America offers its own answer to the Declaration's ideals: a government of the billionaires, by the billionaires and for the billionaires, presiding over armed raids on immigrant communities, a network of concentration camps spreading across the country, children torn from their parents, and the gunning down of unarmed civilians by federal agents on the streets of Minneapolis. Hanging over the proceedings is the stench of Jeffrey Epstein. The Justice Department's release of nearly 3 million pages has shaken the upper tiers of corporate America, exposing connections of billionaires, chief executives and senior political figures to a convicted child sex trafficker. The files, as WSWS Chairman David North recently wrote, "reveal the social physiognomy of a degenerate ruling class and oligarchical society in an advanced state of decomposition. Their offenses are rank; they smell to heaven." Trump himself is deeply implicated. Attorney General Pam Bondi responded to questions about the ongoing cover-up by boasting that the Dow had hit 50,000. FBI documents reveal that billionaire Leslie Wexner was identified as a co-conspirator in 2019, yet no charges have been brought. Emails show Epstein comparing the girls he trafficked to "shrimp—you throw away the head and keep the body." This is the ruling class that governs America. Consider the facts that will be buried beneath Trump's demagogy. The national debt stands at $38.4 trillion, or $113,000 for every person in the country, and is increasing at $8 billion per day. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) projects the deficit at $1.9 trillion this year, growing to $3.1 trillion by 2036, with debt reaching 120 percent of GDP, which is higher than at any point in the nation's history. The dollar fell more than 9 percent in 2025, its worst annual performance since 2017, and remains in bear market territory as BRICS nations increase local currency trade settlements from 35 to 50 percent. Cumulative price increases since 2020 have been devastating: food up more than 25 percent, housing costs still climbing at 3 percent annually, natural gas up nearly 10 percent. The level of social inequality is historically unprecedented. The top 1 percent of households now control 31.7 percent of all wealth, the highest share since the Fed began tracking such data in 1989. They hold $55 trillion, nearly as much as the bottom 90 percent combined. The combined wealth of 935 American billionaires surged to $8.1 trillion at the end of 2025. Elon Musk, Trump's partner in dismantling the federal government through DOGE, has seen his fortune balloon to $726 billion. CEO compensation at S&P 500 companies averaged $18.9 million—285 times the typical worker's pay, up from 21-to-1 in 1965. CEO pay has risen 1,094 percent since 1978, while worker pay has increased just 26 percent. Tesla has proposed a $1 trillion pay package for Musk, exceeding the total annual compensation of the entire federal workforce. The stock market is not a measure of national prosperity. It is a barometer of ruling class enrichment. But the most sinister dimension of the real state of the union is the deployment of armed federal agents against the population. In December 2025, the administration launched Operation Metro Surge, deploying 3,000 masked ICE and Border Patrol agents into the Twin Cities, Minnesota, in what the DHS itself called the largest immigration operation in American history. On January 7, ICE shot and killed Renée Good, a 37-year-old American citizen and mother of three. On January 24, Border Patrol agents shot and killed Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, firing as many as 10 rounds into him as he lay on the ground. The administration has built a network of detention camps across the country, holding nearly 66,000 people—a 75 percent increase, the highest level in history. Congress has allocated $45 billion for still more facilities, with capacity projected at 135,000. Children are separated from their parents at a record rate. A tent camp at Fort Bliss, Texas, holds 5,000 people on the same ground that served as an internment camp for Japanese Americans during World War II. 2025 was the deadliest year for ICE detention on record. The Democrats will offer no genuine opposition. Whatever gestures they make Tuesday night will be meaningless stunts. In actual practice, they collaborate with Trump on the issues that matter most to the ruling class. Where their differences with Trump exist, they are centered on tactical questions of imperialist strategy—above all, dissatisfaction with his insufficient support for the US-NATO proxy war against Russia in Ukraine. But on the drive toward war with Iran, the two parties are in essential agreement. The Democrats' selection of CIA veterans—Senator Elissa Slotkin last year, Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger this year—to deliver the party's response to Trump's State of the Union is itself an assurance to the ruling class that they will uphold the military-intelligence apparatus while using "left"-sounding demagogy from Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez to block the emergence of a mass movement from below. This is the real state of Trump's America in the 250th year since the Declaration of Independence: a society riven by class antagonism on a scale not seen since the Gilded Age, ruled by a criminal oligarchy that has dispensed with even the pretense of democratic governance, lurching toward war abroad and dictatorship at home. But there is another side to the equation, and it is the decisive one. The same crisis driving the ruling class toward fascism and war is propelling the working class into struggle. A developing strike wave, the mass anti-ICE protests, last year's "No Kings" marches, the ongoing walkouts by high school students—these are the initial expressions of a social force that neither party of big business can contain. The critical question is one of political program, perspective and organization. The opposition to the Trump administration cannot be entrusted to the Democrats, who are complicit in everything he does. It must be based on the independent mobilization of the working class. Workers must form rank-and-file committees in every workplace, school and neighborhood to coordinate resistance, demand an end to the ICE terror, defend immigrants, and prepare for a general strike against the government's program of war, austerity and dictatorship. The building of such committees, organized on the basis of a socialist and internationalist program, is the only realistic strategy for defeating the fascistic conspiracy of the American oligarchy. --- ### "US begins pulling staff from Beirut embassy amid Iran attack buildup" — by Andre Damon Published February 24, 2026. Damon reports the State Department ordered 32-50 non-essential staff and family members evacuated from the US Embassy in Beirut, updated its Lebanon travel advisory to "do not travel," and imposed travel restrictions on remaining personnel. This follows the identical pre-strike pattern established before Operation Midnight Hammer in June 2025. The USS Gerald R. Ford — the world's largest warship — arrived at Souda Bay, Crete, and is expected off Israel within days. The Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group is in the Arabian Sea. More than 40,000 US personnel are now in the region. ## What we're covering today - **State of the Union:** Trump delivered a 108-minute address centering the drive toward war with Iran, doubling down on immigration crackdowns, and burying social reality beneath false economic claims. - **Iran:** A third round of Geneva talks is set for Thursday as countries issue urgent evacuation warnings and student protests spread to more universities. - **AI and the military:** The Pentagon gave Anthropic a three-day ultimatum to grant unrestricted military access to Claude AI or face blacklisting under the Defense Production Act. - **Labor:** Tom Hall's Perspective exposes the coordinated sabotage of the Kaiser Permanente strike as part of a broader suppression of the emerging West Coast strike wave. - **Quakertown 5:** The five teenagers arrested at an anti-ICE walkout now face felony aggravated assault charges while the police chief who attacked them faces none. - **ICE protests:** Hundreds protested in Detroit's suburbs against new detention centers; the SEP intervened with a call for a general strike and the abolition of ICE. - **Public health:** Measles cases surged past 1,000 for 2026 as South Carolina engages in deliberate obfuscation of hospitalization data. --- ## Top stories ### 1. Trump's State of the Union: 108 minutes of demagogy, Iran war threats, and Democratic complicity Donald Trump delivered the longest State of the Union address in modern history on the night of February 24 — approximately 108 minutes of belligerent demagogy that centered the drive toward war with Iran, doubled down on the immigration crackdown, and buried the catastrophic social reality of American life beneath transparently false economic claims. On Iran, Trump struck his most aggressive public posture to date. He accused Tehran of working to rebuild its nuclear program after the June 2025 US strikes (Operation Midnight Hammer), claimed Iran is developing missiles "that soon would be capable of reaching the US," and asserted that Iran killed 32,000 antigovernment protesters — a figure far exceeding any credible estimate and immediately denounced by Tehran as "big lies." Trump declared: "My preference is to solve this problem through diplomacy, but one thing is certain: I will never allow the world's number one sponsor of terror to have a nuclear weapon." This formulation — the same language used before the 2003 Iraq invasion — lays the rhetorical groundwork for military action under the guise of a diplomatic failure that Washington itself is engineering. The speech was a festival of false claims. Trump stated gasoline is "below $2.30 a gallon in most states" — not a single state has an average below $2.30, according to AAA. He described inflation as "plummeting" — PolitiFact rated this an exaggeration; the January 2026 year-over-year increase was 2.4%, modestly below the rate Trump inherited. Groceries are up 2%, electricity up 6.3%, housing up 3.4%, and medical care up 3.2% under his tenure. As the WSWS Editorial Board established in yesterday's Perspective, "The real state of Trump's America," the facts Trump will never cite include: national debt at $38.4 trillion growing at $8 billion per day; the top 1% controlling 31.7% of all wealth; CEO pay 285 times average worker pay; and 66,000 people held in ICE detention. On immigration, Trump called on legislators to stand if they believe "the first duty of the American government is to protect American citizens, not illegal aliens," producing a prolonged Republican ovation while Democrats sat. Representative Al Green was escorted out after holding a sign reading "Black people aren't apes," referencing a Trump Truth Social video depicting the Obama family as primates — later removed; the White House called it "posted in error." Dozens of Democrats boycotted the address entirely, attending a rally on the National Mall. The Democratic response was delivered by Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger — a CIA veteran, continuing the pattern from last year when CIA veteran Elissa Slotkin was selected, signaling the Democrats' fealty to the military-intelligence apparatus. Notably absent from the 108-minute address: any acknowledgment of the fourth anniversary of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, despite Trump's campaign promise to end the war; any mention of the Epstein files, though some of Epstein's victims were in attendance. The WSWS has not yet published a dedicated analysis of the speech itself — this is the most significant coverage gap of the day and should be discussed by the board.
Sources AP Trump delivers combative State of the Union centered on Iran and immigration · Al Jazeera Fact-checking Trump's State of the Union claims · PolitiFact Fact-checking Trump's 2026 State of the Union
### 2. Iran war drive escalates: Geneva talks, mass evacuations, student protests The Iran crisis has entered a new phase in the 24 hours since yesterday's briefing. Three developments are significant. First, a third round of indirect US-Iran talks is scheduled for Thursday, February 26, in Geneva. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and his delegation have already departed Tehran. Iran has publicly stressed its "red lines": zero enrichment and the ballistic missile program are non-negotiable, and Iran insists its nuclear program is exclusively civilian. The talks will occur under the toxic atmosphere created by Trump's SOTU claims — including the fabricated 32,000-protester death toll — meaning the diplomatic track is being poisoned even as it nominally proceeds. This is the same dynamic the WSWS's Andre Damon identified in his buildup reporting: the appearance of diplomacy as political cover for the continuation of military preparations. Second, a growing list of countries have issued urgent evacuation warnings for their citizens in Iran. Australia has told its nationals to "leave as soon as possible." Germany describes the "security situation" as "extremely volatile." India has warned all citizens — students, pilgrims, business owners — to "exercise due caution" and leave. Poland's PM Tusk issued a blunt directive: "please leave Iran immediately." South Korea has issued similar warnings. Australia has additionally directed dependents of officials to leave Lebanon and Israel and is offering voluntary departures from Jordan, Qatar, and the UAE. This is not routine diplomatic precaution — it is the international community's intelligence assessment that war is imminent, expressed through civilian evacuation orders. Third, BBC-verified video shows Iranian student protests spreading to additional universities. This is a significant political development that the WSWS has not yet analyzed. The class character of the Iranian student movement, its relationship to the theocratic regime, and the way US imperialist pressure both fuels internal opposition and simultaneously undermines its progressive content — these are questions demanding our analysis. The US is exploiting Iranian internal dissent to justify military intervention, just as it did in Libya and Iraq.
Sources Al Jazeera Third round of indirect US-Iran talks set for Geneva · BBC Iranian student protests spread to more universities
### 3. Pentagon issues ultimatum to Anthropic: AI safeguards or blacklisting As this briefing's author reported for the WSWS, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth gave Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei a three-day ultimatum on Tuesday to grant the military "unfettered access" to Claude AI or face blacklisting as a "supply chain risk" — or compulsion under the Defense Production Act, a wartime industrial law never previously used against an AI company. The confrontation, which took place in a tense meeting attended by six senior defense officials including the Pentagon's top lawyer, was triggered by revelations that Claude was used — without Anthropic's knowledge — in the illegal January 3 military assault on Caracas, Venezuela, in which between 83 and 100 people were killed and President Maduro was abducted. The bourgeois press is framing this as a principled stand by Anthropic for "AI safety" against military overreach. This is a fabrication. As the WSWS article documents, Anthropic has aggressively pursued military integration: a November 2024 partnership with Palantir and AWS to deploy Claude on classified networks; a June 2025 "Claude Gov" product for national security agencies; a $200 million Pentagon contract awarded July 2025. On the same day as the ultimatum, Elon Musk's xAI signed a deal to deploy Grok AI on classified military networks without restriction, breaking Claude's exclusive position — making the Pentagon's leverage over Anthropic explicit. The broader context is Hegseth's January 9 "Artificial Intelligence Strategy for the Department of War," which commits to an "AI-first warfighting force" with seven "Pace-Setting Projects" due for demonstration by July 2026: "Swarm Forge" (autonomous drone swarms), "Agent Network" (AI battle management and kill chain automation), and "Ender's Foundry" (AI military simulation). Anthropic's post-meeting statement expressed "appreciation" for the Department's work and "thanked the Secretary for his service" — the posture of a corporation preparing to capitulate. A senior Defense official told Axios: "The only reason we're still talking to these people is we need them and we need them now."
Sources WSWS Pentagon gives Anthropic 3 days to drop AI safeguards or face blacklisting
### 4. Kaiser Permanente strike betrayal: the political anatomy of coordinated sabotage Tom Hall's Perspective, discussed in the contribution above, provides the essential framework for understanding the UNAC/UHCP bureaucracy's shutdown of the 31,000-worker Kaiser Permanente strike. The strike was ended without a contract, without a membership vote, and on the same day 500 operating engineers were to join — at the precise moment when a potential West Coast strike wave of over 100,000 workers was taking shape. As Hall writes, "A unified movement would have laid the basis for a powerful strike wave across the West Coast, encouraging similar action throughout the country. The bureaucracy could not allow this because it would threaten their financial and social interests." Hall situates the Kaiser betrayal in its full political context. The UNAC/UHCP participates in the Labor-Management Partnership, "funded by management with millions of dollars annually" and "explicitly established in 1997 to prevent strikes and foster a 'culture of collaboration.'" During the strike, Kaiser implicitly threatened this framework through a lawsuit, effectively threatening the bureaucracy with a funding cutoff if it failed to end the action. The corporation reported $9.3 billion in net income while CEO Greg Adams received $13 million in compensation, and had recently settled with the DOJ for $556 million in Medicare fraud. Kaiser maintains investments in ICE detention contractors and has entered a partnership with Walter Reed for mass-casualty war training — the structural integration of the healthcare industry with the military-industrial complex. The Perspective names Democratic politicians as direct agents of suppression: Pelosi and millionaire Mayor Lurie brokering the San Francisco teachers' sellout; "democratic socialist" Mayor Mamdani and Julie Su working behind the scenes on the NewYork-Presbyterian settlement while police arrested protesting nurses; the USW simultaneously pushing through a 30,000-worker refinery sellout that Hall identifies as a war contract keeping fuel flowing for Iran operations. Hall's political conclusion is definitive: "No amount of 'pressure' can alter the social interests of this layer any more than pressure can induce corporate management to give up profits."

### 5. Quakertown 5: students face felony charges while police chief faces none The Quakertown, Pennsylvania situation has escalated significantly. Jacob Crosse reports that the five teenagers arrested during the February 20 anti-ICE walkout now face felony aggravated assault charges — confirmed by the Philadelphia Inquirer from two anonymous sources. Two students remain in custody as of Tuesday; three have been released to parents on house arrest. No charges have been filed against Police Chief Scott McElree, who was filmed in plain clothes choking a teenage girl and assaulting students on a public sidewalk. Attorney Ettore Angelo reported his 15-year-old client was released to house arrest — she faces aggravated assault, a felony punishable by up to five years. Nearly 70 residents packed a typically sparse Quakertown Borough Council work session Monday night demanding McElree's immediate suspension. Student Colin told reporters: "I do not feel very safe going back to school tomorrow... every police officer that was present on the scene that day should be investigated." As Crosse writes, the contrast is stark: these students face five years in prison; Trump has served zero jail time for any of his crimes. ### 7. Measles surges past 1,000 cases — South Carolina engaged in deliberate obfuscation US measles cases have passed the 1,000 mark for 2026 — CDC confirmed 982 as of reporting, with the CNN tracker at 1,030 — far ahead of the pace that produced a three-decade high of 2,281 infections in all of 2025. The US did not breach 1,000 cases until May last year; the threshold has been crossed in late February this time. As Benjamin Mateus reports for the WSWS, South Carolina has emerged as the epicenter, with 973 cases concentrated in Spartanburg County, and the state is engaged in what Dr. Paul Offit of Children's Hospital of Philadelphia describes as deliberate obfuscation: reporting only a 2% hospitalization rate (just 20 admissions) when the CDC's standard estimate for measles hospitalization is 20%. ProPublica documented the cover-up as "driven by profound and calculated indifference." State epidemiologist Dr. Linda Bell admitted the agency "is not getting an accurate picture at all" yet declared the state has not considered adding measles hospitalizations to mandatory reporting. The CDC is rudderless. The principal deputy director, Ralph Abraham — a former Republican congressman — resigned effective immediately "to attend to 'unforeseen family obligations,'" leaving the agency further depleted. Jim O'Neill, the acting director before Bhattacharya, is expected to be nominated to lead the National Science Foundation — an institution for which he is equally unqualified. Bhattacharya's failure to demand transparent hospitalization data from South Carolina constitutes, as Mateus writes, "tacit federal endorsement of the state's obfuscation." National kindergarten MMR vaccine coverage has fallen to 92.5% — below the 95% threshold for herd immunity. The absence of leadership is not a policy failure but the intended policy itself.

The WSWS characterized the talks as a "mafia shakedown." - **Ukraine:** Russia launched overnight missile and drone attacks across Ukraine, wounding at least 25, as US–Ukraine talks proceeded simultaneously in Geneva in the war's fifth year. - **Epstein files:** DOJ accused of withholding FBI interview notes alleging Trump sexually assaulted a 13-year-old girl; Bill Gates admitted affairs with Russian women; Larry Summers quit Harvard. - **Labor:** 30,000 LAUSD support workers voted 94 percent to authorize a strike, joining 35,000 teachers already authorized — putting 65,000 workers on the verge of a walkout in the nation's second-largest school district. - **Palestine:** BBC investigation revealed Israeli soldiers shot a 14-year-old boy in the West Bank and blocked ambulances for 45 minutes while he bled; Israel sent a convoy of 30 vehicles into Syria's Quneitra province. - **AI markets:** A Citrini Research report arguing AI could be "bearish" for tech stocks triggered an 800-point Dow drop, exposing the speculative fragility of the AI investment boom. - **Minnesota Medicaid:** Trump administration froze $259 million in Medicaid funding to Minnesota one day after Trump's racist SOTU attack on the state's Somali community — healthcare access wielded as political punishment. --- ## Top stories ### 1. Trump delivers longest-ever State of the Union, threatens Iran war and announces global tariffs Trump delivered a nearly two-hour State of the Union address on the evening of February 24 — the longest ever — in what the SEP characterized as "a grotesque and degrading spectacle." The speech combined explicit threats of military action against Iran, a sweeping new tariff proposal, racist attacks on Minnesota's Somali community, celebration of the military invasion of Venezuela, and the complete erasure of Gaza and the Palestinian people from any mention throughout the address. On trade, Trump announced a worldwide 10 percent tariff via Section 122 authority after the Supreme Court blocked his previous sweeping tariff policy. He demanded tariffs eventually replace income tax as America's primary revenue source — a proposal that would shift the tax burden decisively onto working-class consumers while relieving corporations and the wealthy of direct taxation. Canada's finance minister Francois-Philippe Champagne responded immediately that the US is "unlikely to lift tariffs," signaling that trade conflict between the two countries will deepen. On domestic policy, Trump unveiled the "Save America Act" to bar "illegal aliens and others" from voting — a measure the SEP described as "a blueprint for voter suppression, intimidation and the criminalization of opposition." He described the military invasion of Venezuela as a "triumph" without mentioning civilian casualties or the seizure of a sovereign nation's government. The word "Palestinian" did not appear once in his speech; Gaza was entirely absent, even as the humanitarian catastrophe there continues. Trump devoted the final 30 minutes of the address to threatening military action against Iran, calling Iran's regime "the world's number one sponsor of terrorism." The WSWS compared this language to the WMD fabrications that preceded the 2003 Iraq invasion, noting that the rhetorical escalation is designed to prepare the American public for a war that has never been meaningfully debated. Trump used explicitly racist language against Minnesota's Somali community, describing them as "pirates" who have "pillaged" tens of billions of dollars. The SEP statement called this the same language "the Nazis used against Jews as they laid the groundwork for the Holocaust." He also celebrated the deaths of Renee Nicole Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis without naming them or acknowledging the circumstances. Representative Al Green (D-TX) was physically removed from the chamber for holding a protest sign. No Democrat objected. The SEP statement observed that "the Democrats fear that if they do anything that undermines the obsolete procedures of congressional decorum, it will encourage popular opposition and mass working class action." The Democratic response was delivered by Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger, an ex-CIA officer, who criticized Trump for "ceding economic power and technological strength to China" but did not mention the invasion of Venezuela, Gaza, the Epstein files, or ICE killings. Key facts: - BBC fact-check: US inflation at 2.4 percent in 12 months to January 2026, down from 3 percent at end of Biden term - Trump invoked Section 122 authority for the new global 10 percent tariff after the Supreme Court blocked his previous tariff program - Republican Congress members chanted "USA, USA!" throughout — characterized by the SEP as "the American equivalent of 'Sieg Heil'" - Democrats offered no substantive opposition to any element of the speech

### 7. AI fears trigger 800-point Dow drop after Citrini Research report A research report by Citrini Research circulated widely on February 25 and contributed to sharp market losses: the Dow fell over 800 points (1.7 percent), the S&P 500 dropped 1 percent, and the NASDAQ fell 1.1 percent. The report posed a question that struck at the heart of the AI investment thesis: "What if our AI bullishness continues to be right ... and what if that's actually bearish?" The argument: AI productivity tools could massively reduce labor costs and time, destroying the valuations of existing software and information companies — including the very tech giants spending trillions on AI data centers. The Wall Street Journal noted the report "tapped into a new strain of fears about AI." The core concern is whether hyperscaler spending by Meta, Google, Amazon, and Microsoft will generate sufficient returns, or whether the technology they are building will undermine the profit structures on which their valuations rest. The market reaction illustrates the extreme speculative instability of the AI investment boom, where trillions of dollars in capital expenditure rest on assumptions about future returns that even AI proponents now acknowledge may not materialize. As the WSWS noted, the technology may succeed in its stated purpose — increasing productivity — while simultaneously destroying the profit basis of the companies investing in it, a contradiction rooted in the capitalist mode of production itself. The Citrini report's core insight is that AI may be the rare technology that harms its own investors by succeeding too well.

Renewed fighting could involve both TPLF factions and Eritrea, threatening to reignite one of the deadliest conflicts of the 21st century in a region already destabilized by the Sudanese civil war to the northwest. The ceasefire agreement that ended the war has never been fully implemented. Eritrean forces, which were supposed to withdraw from Tigray, remain in disputed areas, and the underlying political and ethnic tensions that fueled the original conflict have not been addressed. The international community's attention has shifted elsewhere, leaving the population exposed to a potential resumption of mass violence.
Sources BBC Ethiopia heading back to war? Tigray on edge
### US allows Venezuelan oil sales to Cuba amid Caribbean humanitarian alarm The US Treasury issued a new "favorable licensing policy" allowing companies to seek licenses to resell Venezuelan oil for "commercial and humanitarian use in Cuba." The move follows alarm from Caribbean neighbors over Cuba's rapidly deteriorating humanitarian crisis caused by fuel shortages. An oil tanker, the Nicos IV, was photographed in the Port of Matanzas, Cuba. The policy excludes entities associated with Cuba's military or intelligence services. Cuba's health system has been in deepening crisis since Venezuela cut oil supplies after the US tightened sanctions — a chain of consequences in which US sanctions policy against one country cascades through dependent economies. Cuba relied on Venezuelan oil for decades under preferential terms, and the collapse of that arrangement has left the island unable to maintain basic medical services, electricity generation, and transportation. Rolling blackouts lasting 10-16 hours are common in many provinces. The "favorable licensing policy" represents a partial acknowledgment by Washington that its sanctions architecture is producing a humanitarian emergency in the Caribbean — while maintaining the legal framework that caused it.
Sources Al Jazeera US to allow Venezuelan oil sales to Cuba as alarm grows in the Caribbean
### Near-blind Rohingya refugee found dead in Buffalo after Border Patrol abandoned him Nurul Amin Shah Alam, 56, a nearly blind Rohingya refugee from Myanmar's Rakhine state, was found dead on a Buffalo, New York street on Tuesday evening. He had been missing since February 19, when US Border Patrol agents dropped him at a coffee shop far from his home after releasing him from county jail. Family members and advocacy groups say agents had no plan for his safe return. Shah Alam survived genocide in Myanmar's Rakhine state — where the Rohingya have been subjected to mass killings, rape, and forced displacement since 2017 — only to die abandoned on an American street in winter. His near-blindness meant he could not navigate an unfamiliar city on his own, a fact the Border Patrol agents who dropped him at a coffee shop would have been aware of. His death is a concrete illustration of the lethal consequences of an immigration enforcement system that processes human beings as administrative units rather than people requiring care. Family advocacy groups are demanding accountability for what they describe as a foreseeable and preventable death.
Sources Al Jazeera Near-blind Rohingya refugee dies after US agents left him far from home

Together, 65,000 LAUSD employees — bus drivers, cafeteria workers, custodians, classroom aides, and teachers — are poised to strike in the nation's second-largest school district. The average classified worker salary is $35,501, classified as "extremely low income" in Los Angeles, where the cost of living far exceeds that figure. Ninety percent are Latino or African American, and many are immigrants. The LAUSD struggle is part of a broader wave of labor conflict in California: 40,000 UC graduate workers are also set to strike when their contract expires March 31, 35,000 Kaiser healthcare workers recently waged a month-long strike, and CSU skilled trades workers are also striking. one dead was an American citizen ### 1. Pakistan declares "open war" on Afghanistan, bombs Kabul Pakistan launched air strikes on Afghanistan's capital Kabul and the provinces of Kandahar and Paktika in the early hours of Friday, after the Afghan Taliban announced "large-scale offensive operations" against Pakistani military posts along the Durand Line at 20:00 local time Thursday. Pakistan's Defence Minister Khawaja Asif declared the two countries are now at "open war." Two Taliban brigade headquarters were reportedly destroyed. Taliban drones struck three Pakistani locations in response. This is the most significant military escalation between the two nuclear-armed neighbors since at least 2021. The October 2025 ceasefire — agreed after a week of deadly clashes — has completely collapsed. The conflict is rooted in the disputed 2,600 km Durand Line, a colonial-era border drawn by British imperialism in 1893 that Afghanistan has never recognized. Key facts: - Pakistan claims at least 70 Taliban fighters killed in its strikes - Defence Minister Asif: "Our cup of patience has overflowed. Now it is open war between us and you" - UN Secretary-General Guterres expressed "deep concern" and called for protection of civilians - Iran's Foreign Minister Araghchi — in Geneva for the nuclear talks — called for dialogue between the two countries - Both states possess nuclear weapons; Pakistan's arsenal is estimated at 170 warheads
Sources BBC Pakistan declares open war on Afghanistan, bombs Kabul · Al Jazeera Pakistan bombs Kabul: Why are Afghanistan and Pakistan fighting? · Al Jazeera World reacts to eruption of fighting between Pakistan and Afghanistan
### 2. US-Iran nuclear talks conclude in Geneva amid war buildup The first round of US-Iran nuclear talks in Geneva concluded Thursday with Oman's Foreign Minister Albusaidi saying "significant progress" had been made and further technical and high-level talks would occur next week. Iranian FM Araghchi claimed "good progress" and described a "serious examination of the elements of an agreement." The US side made no comment after the second, shorter session — described by Western media as "ominous." As Keith Jones argued in the WSWS Perspective, were Iran to comply, it would be rendered "effectively defenceless in the face of US or Israeli aggression" — constituting de facto imperialist-imposed regime change. ### 7. Cuba repels armed attack from US-flagged speedboat Cuba's border guards repelled a US-flagged, Florida-registered speedboat carrying weapons, explosives, and heavily armed Cuban exiles roughly one nautical mile off the Villa Clara coast. Four men were killed and six wounded. One of the dead was an American citizen. Assets seized included assault rifles, pistols, Molotov cocktails, explosive devices, ballistic vests, telescopic sights, and camouflage uniforms. A seventh detainee reportedly confessed to having been sent from the US to make advance preparations. Key facts: - The speedboat was Florida-registered (FL7726SH) and carried weapons, explosives, and trained operatives - Most identified attackers are Cuban nationals residing in the US; some are on Cuba's list of wanted terrorists - The attack occurred during Rubio's Caricom summit appearance

--- ## Science, technology, and public health - [US measles emergency deepens](#us-measles-emergency-deepens) — 982 confirmed cases as of Feb 19 across 26 jurisdictions; 89% outbreak-associated; on pace to dwarf 2025's three-decade record of 2,281 - [Vegetarians have substantially lower risk of five cancer types](#vegetarians-have-substantially-lower-risk-of-five-cancer-types) — Oxford study of 1.8 million people finds 28% lower kidney cancer risk and 31% lower multiple myeloma risk - [Stem cells in utero for spina bifida: "major milestone" trial](#stem-cells-in-utero-for-spina-bifida) — first trial shows placental stem cell therapy during fetal surgery is safe and improves child mobility - [New GLP-1 pill achieves up to 8% body weight loss](#new-glp-1-pill-achieves-up-to-8-body-weight-loss) — Eli Lilly's orforglipron outperforms existing semaglutide tablets in clinical trial - [First writing may be 40,000 years older than thought](#first-writing-may-be-40000-years-older-than-thought) — researchers identify meaningful patterns in 45,000-year-old mammoth tusk carvings ### US measles emergency deepens The CDC's measles tracker shows 982 confirmed cases in the United States as of February 19, 2026, across 26 jurisdictions, with 7 new outbreaks this year and 89% of cases (870) outbreak-associated. The total has likely surpassed 1,000 by late February, putting 2026 on pace to far exceed 2025's three-decade record of 2,281 confirmed cases and three deaths (two Texas children, one New Mexico adult). This emergency formed the backdrop to the Senate HELP Committee confirmation hearing for Casey Means — Trump and Kennedy's nominee for surgeon general — who refused to recommend measles vaccination to parents, declined to rule out a vaccine-autism link despite decades of refuting science, and defended past attacks on birth control. If confirmed, Means would become the first surgeon general without an active medical license. HHS under Kennedy has already fired 20,000+ scientists and public health workers, proposed a 54% cut to the CDC budget, and slashed the childhood vaccine schedule from 17 to 11 recommended vaccines.
Sources CDC Measles Cases and Outbreaks tracker · WSWS From wellness grifter to surgeon general: Trump nominates anti-science quack Casey Means
### Vegetarians have substantially lower risk of five cancer types A study published in the *British Journal of Cancer*, led by Dr. Aurora Perez-Cornago at the University of Oxford, analyzed data from over 1.8 million people tracked over many years. Vegetarians had 21% lower risk of pancreatic cancer, 12% lower risk of prostate cancer, 9% lower risk of breast cancer, 28% lower risk of kidney cancer, and 31% lower risk of multiple myeloma compared with meat eaters. These five cancers account for approximately 20% of cancer deaths in the UK. This is the largest study of its kind and provides strong population-level evidence for dietary effects on major cancers.
Sources Guardian Vegetarians have 'substantially lower risk' of five types of cancer
### Stem cells in utero for spina bifida A UK clinical trial published Thursday found that applying stem cells from the mother's placenta to the baby's spine during in utero surgery for spina bifida is safe and improves child mobility and quality of life. Doctors described the results as "a major milestone" in the treatment of birth defects. The condition affects approximately 1,500 UK births annually, and this is the first trial demonstrating that stem cell therapy during fetal surgery is both safe and effective — potentially transformative for prenatal medicine.
Sources Guardian Giving stem cells in utero to babies with spina bifida boosts quality of life, trial finds
### New GLP-1 pill achieves up to 8% body weight loss An Eli Lilly clinical trial published Thursday showed that orforglipron, a daily pill for type 2 diabetes, led to greater weight loss than existing semaglutide tablets — up to 8% body weight loss. The drug may offer a more effective non-injection GLP-1 alternative and could expand access to these therapies if approved. It has not yet received regulatory approval in the UK, US, or Europe. The results come as Novo Nordisk's next-generation weight-loss drug was labeled "obsolete" by analysts last week.
Sources Guardian New GLP-1 pill helps patients lose more weight than existing tablets, trial finds
### First writing may be 40,000 years older than thought Researchers at the University of Saarland and the Berlin Museum analyzed over 3,000 characters on 260 objects — including 45,000-year-old mammoth tusks from German caves — and found patterns of meaning in lines, notches, dots, and crosses that they describe as "the DNA of writing." Traditional historiography dates the first writing to approximately 3,000 BCE Mesopotamian proto-cuneiform. If confirmed, this pushes the origin of symbolic communication back by 40,000 years, to the period when Homo sapiens moved from Africa to Europe and interacted with Neanderthals.
Sources BBC First writing may be 40,000 years older than thought
--- ## Arts and culture - [Berlinale closes with Gaza controversy](#berlinale-closes-with-gaza-controversy-as-artists-refuse-to-speak) — Palestinian director called Germany a "partner in genocide" in acceptance speech; state broadcaster accused him of "hate speech"; Wenders and Paul Thomas Anderson both refused to comment on the war - [Warner Bros-Paramount merger threatens cultural production](#warner-bros-paramount-merger-and-the-crisis-of-cultural-production) — $111bn deal accelerates Hollywood's consolidation as SAG-AFTRA negotiations face an industry in contraction - ['Think of England' trailer: WWII satire of British militarism](#think-of-england-wwii-satire-trailer) — new film satirizes wartime propaganda through the lens of morale-boosting pornographic films - [Eric Dane tribute on Grey's Anatomy](#eric-dane-tribute-on-greys-anatomy) — the show paid tribute to the late actor in an emotional episode ### Berlinale closes with Gaza controversy as artists refuse to speak The closing ceremony of the Berlin International Film Festival became the site of the most significant cultural confrontation over Gaza in the European festival circuit. Palestinian director Abdallah Alkhatib used his acceptance speech to call the German government a "partner in genocide" — a characterization that 62% of German citizens agree with, according to polling. German state broadcaster ARD accused Alkhatib of "hate speech" and labeled the moment a "scandal." Far-right culture minister Wolfram Weimer rejected what he called a "malicious false accusation." The Berlinale controversy laid bare the role of bourgeois cultural institutions in maintaining the ideological framework that enables the ongoing destruction of Gaza. The German ruling class, which has made unconditional support for Israel a pillar of its "state reason" (*Staatsrason*), cannot tolerate artistic expression that names the reality of its complicity. ARD's reflexive labeling of a Palestinian filmmaker's speech as "hate speech" demonstrates how the language of liberal tolerance is weaponized to silence opposition to imperialist violence. More telling was the response of prominent Western filmmakers. Ethan Hawke said Gaza was "not my agenda." Both Wim Wenders and Paul Thomas Anderson — the latter at the BAFTAs — used nearly identical language: "We are not politicians." This claim to political neutrality in the midst of genocide is itself a political act. It places these artists in the tradition not of courageous artistic independence but of accommodation to power. The contrast with Alkhatib, who risked his career to speak, is stark. ### Warner Bros-Paramount merger and the crisis of cultural production The $111bn Paramount Skydance takeover of Warner Bros Discovery, cleared by Netflix's withdrawal from the bidding, represents an extraordinary concentration of cultural production in fewer and fewer corporate hands. The merger would combine two of Hollywood's most historically significant studios — Warner Bros (founded 1923) and Paramount (founded 1912) — under a single corporate umbrella. California AG Rob Bonta's warning that it may face antitrust review offers thin reassurance given the regulatory trend. This consolidation is the economic context for SAG-AFTRA's current contract negotiations with the AMPTP, which began February 9. Tens of thousands of industry workers have lost jobs in what the trade press calls the "Great Hollywood Contraction," and entire crafts face elimination by AI. The union leadership has announced that negotiations will be held "behind closed doors with complete media silence," leaving members in the dark. ### Think of England: WWII satire trailer A trailer was released for "Think of England," a British WWII satire in which the armed forces shoot morale-boosting pornographic films. The film is described as a satire of British imperialism and militarism — notable timing given the broader European rearmament drive and the rhetorical invocations of wartime spirit accompanying it. ### Eric Dane tribute on Grey's Anatomy Grey's Anatomy aired an emotional tribute to Eric Dane, who died recently, featuring a video montage set to "Chasing Cars." Dane played the character of "McSteamy" and was a significant figure in the long-running medical drama.
