--- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Left Voice (PTS/FT)](#left-voice-ptsft) — covers Berlinale and ICE resistance; frames political questions as matters of individual moral courage rather than class analysis; promotes local reform campaigns as substitutes for independent working-class politics - [Liberation News / PSL](#liberation-news--psl) — demands freedom for CPM-K leader Omole and comments on Epstein; calls for socialism without any strategy for achieving it; treats allied Stalinist parties as genuinely Marxist - [Socialist Alternative (ISA/CWI)](#socialist-alternative-isacwi) — issues general strike call for May 1 without addressing the union bureaucracies that would suppress it; frames Trump as "Trumpism in crisis" rather than fascist consolidation of state power - [SWP UK (Socialist Worker)](#swp-uk-socialist-worker) — celebrates Green by-election win as "anti-racist victory" using identity-politics framing that avoids the class character of the Green Party; reports on zero-hours contracts without a program beyond pressuring Labour - [Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT)](#socialist-appeal--rcp-imt) — focuses on British parliamentary maneuvers and self-promotional recruitment reports; no coverage of workers' struggles in this period ### Left Voice (PTS/FT) Left Voice published three notable pieces. On the **Berlinale closing ceremony**, Nathaniel Flakin correctly identified the significance of Palestinian director Abdallah Alkhatib's "partner in genocide" speech and the cowardice of Wenders and Paul Thomas Anderson in refusing to comment on Gaza. However, the article frames the question entirely in terms of individual moral courage versus cowardice — it asks why these artists will not speak up, rather than analyzing the class character of bourgeois film culture or the role of the German social-democratic state in enabling genocide. The structural complicity of European cultural institutions with imperialism goes unexamined. On **ICE in Metro Detroit**, Left Voice calls for a local campaign to "kick ICE out" of the area, "building on the lessons of Minneapolis." The article proposes a coalition and pressure campaign but does not challenge the Democratic Party apparatus that administers Detroit, nor does it offer an independent class strategy. Similarly, coverage of **UAW Local 2325's strike vote against ICE** presents a single local's resolution as a model for the labor movement — without addressing the UAW International leadership's role in systematically suppressing rank-and-file opposition, which is the central focus of the WSWS's coverage through Will Lehman's campaign. Left Voice's overall orientation: correct identification of significant political moments (Gaza, ICE raids) combined with a consistent refusal to draw class conclusions or challenge the political frameworks (Democratic Party, union bureaucracy) that contain working-class opposition.
Sources Left Voice Berlinale Film Festival: 'I'm not a politician' · Left Voice We need a campaign to kick ICE out of Metro Detroit · Left Voice UAW Local 2325 commits to a strike vote to fight ICE
### Liberation News / PSL The PSL issued a **statement demanding the release of Booker Ngesa Omole**, leader of the Communist Party Marxist-Kenya (CPM-K), jailed on what the PSL characterizes as fraudulent charges. The statement calls for international solidarity — a correct impulse — but characterizes Omole's party as "Marxist" without examining the CPM-K's actual political program or its relationship to Kenyan nationalism. Notably, the WSWS also covered Omole's detention this week, providing an area of convergent coverage. On the **Epstein file revelations**, PSL published "Justice will not be served for Epstein survivors under capitalism," which argues that the capitalist system makes genuine accountability impossible. The conclusion is superficially correct but operates at the level of social-democratic critique of wealth and corruption rather than Marxist analysis. There is no discussion of the specific connections exposed between Epstein, the Israeli state, US intelligence agencies, and the ruling class — precisely the concrete content that would give the abstract "capitalism" critique material force. The article offers no strategy for how workers can achieve the socialist transformation it vaguely invokes. PSL's overall orientation: solidarity statements and systemic critiques that stop at the threshold of concrete political analysis, particularly regarding the state and its intelligence apparatus.
Sources Liberation News PSL statement: Free Booker Ngesa Omole! · Liberation News Justice will not be served for Epstein survivors under capitalism
### Socialist Alternative (ISA/CWI) Socialist Alternative published a **State of the Union analysis** characterizing Trump as promoting "Trumpism in crisis" following the Supreme Court tariff ruling. The article calls for resistance "in the streets" and on May 1 but frames Trump's political program as a crisis of a personal tendency ("Trumpism") rather than the fascist consolidation of capitalist state power. There is no analysis of the Democrats' role in enabling or colluding with Trump's program. More significantly, SAlt published **"The Case for a Nationwide General Strike on May 1"** — listing grievances (ICE raids, Medicaid cuts, union busting) and calling for "labor, environmental, and social movement coalitions." The call is a pressure campaign designed to build the organization's profile, not a concrete revolutionary strategy. It does not address who would lead such a strike, how it would bypass the union bureaucracies that have systematically suppressed rank-and-file militancy, or what political program it would advance. The DSA/SAlt tendency has repeatedly used "strike" rhetoric to channel worker anger into electoral campaigns for the Democratic Party.
Sources Socialist Alternative State of the Union: Trump insists we're in the 'Golden Age of America' · Socialist Alternative The case for a nationwide general strike on May 1
### SWP UK (Socialist Worker) The SWP celebrated the **Gorton and Denton by-election result** as a defeat for Reform UK rather than a defeat for Labour, framing the Green win primarily as an "anti-racist" victory. This is a classic identity-politics framing that avoids the class question entirely. The Green Party is a bourgeois parliamentary party that cannot represent a genuine socialist alternative to Labour; the SWP's celebration reflects its adaptation to left-liberal politics. SWP also reported on **record-high zero-hours contracts** in the UK — correctly identifying "casualisation" and "exploitation" under the Labour government, but proposing only a general pressure campaign on Labour as the solution. No independent political program for the working class is offered.
Sources Socialist Worker Anti-racists celebrate as Reform UK trashed in Gorton and Denton · Socialist Worker Use of zero hours contracts reaches record high
### Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT) The IMT published on the **Chagos Islands deal** collapsing as a political crisis for Starmer, framing it primarily as a parliamentary "blow" rather than analyzing the anti-colonial implications or the role of US-UK imperialism in maintaining Indian Ocean military bases. Separately, the RCP published **organizational self-promotion** reporting recruitment figures — "400 members and rising" at their London Marxist School — with no coverage of actual UK workers' struggles in this period. The IMT tendency combines formal Marxist phraseology with organizational ambition and avoidance of programmatic confrontation with reformism.
Sources Communist.red Chagos Islands deal blows up in Starmer's face · Communist.red Revolutionary Communist Party on the advance

US and Israel launch war on Iran; at least 201 killed, Iran retaliates across the Gulf In the early hours of Saturday February 28, the United States and Israel launched a joint military operation against Iran — dubbed "Lion's Roar" by Netanyahu — constituting the most significant US military strike since the 2003 invasion of Iraq. The EU urged "maximum restraint." Mediator Oman warned the US "not to get sucked in further." - **Trump quotes:** "We're doing this not for now. We're doing this for the future." --- ## International developments - [Japan approves near-total lifting of weapons export ban](#japan-approves-near-total-lifting-of-weapons-export-ban) — no Diet approval required; lethal weapons can now be exported to 17 nations including countries at war, as defense spending hits 2% of GDP - [UK Labour minister resigns over journalists' investigation scandal](#uk-labour-minister-resigns-over-journalists-investigation-scandal) — APCO Worldwide paid £30,000+ to investigate reporters, noting one's "Jewish beliefs" and accusing him of aiding Russian interests - [Modi's Israel visit and the Iran war context](#modis-israel-visit-and-the-iran-war-context) — two-day visit coincided precisely with final preparations for the US-Israeli attack; India has abstained on all major UN Gaza votes - [Pakistan-Afghanistan conflict continues](#pakistan-afghanistan-conflict-continues) — nuclear-armed Pakistan's "open war" declaration against Afghanistan risks being overshadowed by the Iran war ### Japan approves near-total lifting of weapons export ban Japan's LDP Research Commission on Security approved lifting nearly all restrictions on lethal weapons exports — a dramatic escalation of Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's remilitarization agenda following the LDP's landslide in the February general election. Exports of jointly developed weapons to third countries are now permitted, and exports to nations at war are allowed with National Security Council approval. The changes apply to 17 partner nations including the US, UK, Australia, and the Philippines. No Diet approval is required. Japan's defense budget has now reached 2% of GDP. As the WSWS's Ben McGrath noted, Tokyo is "rapidly accelerating its remilitarization agenda in preparation for waging war abroad and war against the working class at home."
Sources WSWS Newly-elected far-right Japanese government prepares war measures
### UK Labour minister resigns over journalists' investigation scandal Labour MP Josh Simons resigned as Cabinet Office minister after an ethics adviser investigation into his tenure at the think tank Labour Together. A contract addressed to Simons also commissioned investigation of journalists Paul Holden and Matt Taibbi. More than 20 Labour MPs had called for an independent investigation. Simons said he had "become a distraction." Labour Together was "widely credited" with helping Starmer win the Labour leadership — this scandal exposes the authoritarian surveillance methods at the heart of Starmer's political machine.

--- ## Science, technology, and public health - [Measles surges to 1,136 cases](#measles-surge-continues-1136-confirmed-cases) — 28 jurisdictions reporting; on pace to surpass 2025's record of 2,281 before end of April; 90% of cases are outbreak-associated - [COVID origins: first comprehensive investigation published](#covid-19-origins-first-comprehensive-investigation-published) — Nature publishes synthesis of evidence from "the world's first comprehensive investigation into how a pandemic started" - [Stem cell therapy for spina bifida in the womb](#medical-advances) — world-first in utero intervention using stem cells shows promise - [CAR T cell therapy breakthrough](#medical-advances) — ultra-sensitive approach eliminates hard-to-treat tumors in mice - [Iran war: global aviation system disrupted](#iran-war-disrupts-global-aviation) — eight countries close airspace; hundreds of thousands stranded ### Measles surge continues: 1,136 confirmed cases As of February 26, the CDC reports 1,136 confirmed measles cases in the United States in 2026, across 28 jurisdictions. Ten new outbreaks have been reported this year, with 90% of cases (1,023 of 1,136) outbreak-associated. For comparison, full year 2025 saw 2,281 cases with 50 outbreaks; 2024 had only 285 cases and 16 outbreaks. The US is on pace to surpass 2025's record total before the end of April — a catastrophic collapse in vaccine-preventable disease control. Six cases were among international visitors. Jay Bhattacharya's control of CDC and NIH has not reversed the anti-vaccination political climate fostered by the Trump administration.
Sources CDC Measles cases and outbreaks data
### COVID-19 origins: first comprehensive investigation published Nature published a comment article titled "COVID's origins: what we do and don't know," summarizing what it calls "key insights from the world's first comprehensive investigation into how a pandemic started." The article provides a synthesis of evidence on the lab leak versus natural spillover debate without reaching a definitive conclusion, noting ongoing scientific debate. This is the first attempt at a comprehensive, peer-reviewed synthesis of COVID origins evidence — directly relevant to the WSWS Global Workers' Inquest into the COVID-19 Pandemic, which has insisted on rigorous scientific investigation as opposed to the politically motivated "lab leak" narrative promoted by the US intelligence establishment.
Sources Nature COVID's origins: what we do and don't know
### Medical advances - **Spina bifida stem cell therapy (Nature, Feb 26):** A world-first in utero stem cell therapy shows promise for treating spina bifida in the womb — a potential breakthrough in prenatal treatment for a congenital condition affecting thousands annually. - **CAR T cell therapy (Nature, Feb 26):** "Ultra-sensitive CAR T cells eliminate hard-to-treat tumours in mice" — a new approach targeting refractory cancers resistant to current CAR T immunotherapy. - **Animal testing alternatives (Nature, Feb 25):** Major news feature on how advances in organ models and computer simulations are raising the prospect of eliminating some animal experiments, though "huge hurdles" remain. Signals a potential scientific and ethical shift in biomedical research methods.
Sources Nature Stem cell therapy shows promise for spina bifida / CAR T cells eliminate tumors / Age of animal experiments
### Iran war disrupts global aviation Eight countries closed their airspace following the US-Israeli attack on Iran. British Airways, Virgin Atlantic, Wizz Air, Qatar Airways, and Emirates suspended or diverted Middle East operations. The UK Foreign Office urged British nationals in Bahrain, Qatar, Kuwait, and the UAE to "immediately shelter in place." Hundreds of thousands of travelers are stranded. This is a direct disruption of civilian infrastructure as a consequence of military aggression.

--- ## Arts and culture - [Berlinale director faces ouster over Gaza row](#berlinale-director-faces-ouster-over-gaza-row) — if Tricia Tuttle is pushed out, the festival may lose all claim to being a genuinely political cultural institution - [David Walsh reviews Chloé Zhao's *Hamnet*](#chloé-zhaos-hamnet-reviewed-by-wsws) — strong performances from Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal, but the film does not fully engage the social forces Shakespeare grappled with - [Bobby J. Brown (*The Wire*) dies in barn fire](#deaths-of-cultural-figures) — actor associated with one of television's most significant social realist works - [Oliver "Power" Grant (Wu-Tang Clan) dies](#deaths-of-cultural-figures) — collaborator connected to hip-hop's most class-conscious artistic movement ### Berlinale director faces ouster over Gaza row The Guardian reports that organizers of the Berlin Film Festival may push out director Tricia Tuttle over "the latest Gaza row," threatening the festival's identity as a genuinely political cultural institution. Berlin's status as a national capital makes it uniquely vulnerable to state pressure on cultural events — the German government has been among the most aggressive in Europe in suppressing pro-Palestinian expression. The article praises Tuttle for maintaining the festival's public-facing character in the face of political censorship. This is part of a broader pattern across European cultural institutions. The German state's equation of criticism of Israel with antisemitism — codified in the Bundestag's 2024 resolution — has created an atmosphere of cultural repression that extends from universities to museums to film festivals. The Berlinale, historically one of the few major festivals willing to screen politically challenging work from the Middle East and Global South, is now under existential pressure. Its potential capitulation would represent a significant victory for the forces seeking to suppress artistic engagement with the genocide in Gaza.
Sources Guardian Berlinale director faces ouster over Gaza row
### Chloé Zhao's *Hamnet* reviewed by WSWS David Walsh reviews Chloé Zhao's film *Hamnet*, based on Maggie O'Farrell's 2020 novel, starring Jessie Buckley as Agnes/Anne Hathaway and Paul Mescal as the young William Shakespeare. The film proposes that Shakespeare wrote *Hamlet* as a tribute to his 11-year-old son Hamnet, who died in 1596. Walsh acknowledges the film's genuine emotional depth, strong performances, and period authenticity — Buckley is particularly compelling — while identifying its central limitation: it does not fully engage with the social and historical forces that Shakespeare himself grappled with in his greatest works. The review's significance extends beyond a single film. Published on the day the United States launched a war on Iran, Walsh's insistence that great art must engage with "the very age and body of the time" — Shakespeare's own phrase from *Hamlet* — resonates with particular urgency. The question of whether artists today are capable of confronting the social catastrophes of their era, rather than retreating into private emotional landscapes, is among the most important facing contemporary culture.
Sources WSWS Hamnet, Hamlet and the demanding effort "to show the very age and body of the time"
### Baz Luhrmann's second Elvis documentary Baz Luhrmann has made a second film about Elvis Presley, this time focused on previously unseen private footage from spring 1972. "Much as I try, I have never been able to follow the system," Luhrmann said. The film is a follow-up to his 2022 biographical feature. Elvis Presley remains one of the most culturally significant and contradictory figures of 20th-century American popular music — his appropriation and transformation of Black musical traditions, his working-class origins, and the machinery of commercial exploitation that ultimately consumed him all warrant ongoing artistic investigation.
Sources Guardian Baz Luhrmann interview on second Elvis documentary
### Deaths of cultural figures - **Bobby J. Brown** (*The Wire*), US actor, died in a barn fire. *The Wire* depicted the structural devastation of deindustrialization on Baltimore's working class and its relationship to the drug trade and police violence — one of the most significant works of social realism in American television history. - **Oliver "Power" Grant**, close collaborator with Wu-Tang Clan throughout the 1990s and early 2000s, has died. Wu-Tang Clan's music emerged from the poverty and social devastation of Staten Island and reflected the lived experience of the working class during the age of deindustrialization — one of hip-hop's most artistically ambitious and class-conscious movements.
Sources Guardian Bobby J. Brown, The Wire actor, dies in barn fire · Guardian Wu-Tang Clan collaborator Oliver "Power" Grant dies
### 2026 Winter Olympics: athleticism and nationalist chauvinism The WSWS's Andy Thompson reviewed the Milano-Cortina Winter Olympics, which closed February 22. The article praises historic athletic achievements — France's Fournier Beaudry and Cizeron took Ice Dance gold; Brazil and Kazakhstan achieved firsts — while analyzing how nationalist chauvinism and the looming threat of war tainted the Games. The Olympics drew 23.5 million daily US viewers, with approximately two-thirds of the total US population watching at least one event — making them the most-watched Winter Games in US history. The contrast between the internationalist spirit of athletic competition and the nationalist poison injected by governments and media is a recurring theme in the WSWS's coverage of major sporting events. Published as the US launches a war on Iran, the analysis acquires additional weight.
Sources WSWS The 2026 Winter Olympics: Remarkable athleticism poisoned by nationalist chauvinism
--- ## WSWS coverage — last 24 hours **Perspective published:** "Mamdani embraces Trump: Collaboration with fascism from the DSA mayor" by the Socialist Equality Party (US) — read in full on wsws.org ### "Oppose the US imperialist war on Iran!" — David North David North's urgent statement, posted to X on the morning of February 28, is the WSWS's immediate response to the US-Israeli attack. North draws a direct historical comparison between Trump's February 28, 2026 attack on Iran and Hitler's September 1, 1939 invasion of Poland, characterizing them as "crimes of equal magnitude." He notes Trump concealed his decision at the SOTU just four days earlier. North identifies the attack as not merely a foreign policy decision but a turning point in world history: "In the future, historians will compare Trump's February 28, 2026 attack on Iran to Hitler's September 1, 1939 invasion of Poland." The statement calls on the working class to "call meetings in your factories, workplaces, schools and neighborhoods demanding the immediate end of this war" — a programmatic call for independent working-class mobilization that distinguishes the ICFI's response from the pseudo-left's "movement" rhetoric.
Sources WSWS Oppose the US imperialist war on Iran!
### "Mamdani embraces Trump: Collaboration with fascism from the DSA mayor" — SEP (US) The Perspective's unique analytical contribution — beyond what is covered in Top Stories above — lies in its historical framework. The SEP traces Jacobin's recent "potted history" of the Popular Front as the DSA's intellectual framework for collaboration, arguing that Jacobin deliberately covers up the Popular Front's treacherous purpose (defending capitalism by suppressing proletarian revolution) and the murderous methods of Stalinism. The operative historical analogy, the SEP argues, is not the Popular Front but the Stalin-Hitler Pact of August 1939 — "the most infamous act of political treachery in the 20th century." Stalin's toast to Hitler is directly compared to Mamdani's photo-op: "I know how much the German nation loves its Führer; I should therefore like to drink to his health." The article develops the Laval analogy: "The argument that Mamdani is 'getting results'... is precisely the argument advanced by Pierre Laval." It concludes with the demand that "the working class and youth must not allow their anger and opposition to be corralled behind the Democratic Party and its political appendages such as the DSA."
Sources WSWS Mamdani embraces Trump: Collaboration with fascism from the DSA mayor
### "Trump blacklists Anthropic, orders all federal agencies to cease use of AI firm's technology" — Evan Blake The WSWS article's unique contribution is its class analysis of the dispute. While the bourgeois press presented Amodei as a principled hero standing up to authoritarianism, Blake demonstrates that the confrontation is over the pace and terms of AI integration into the war machine, not the principle. The article systematically dismantles each element of Amodei's public positioning, showing that his two narrow technical objections (autonomous targeting and domestic surveillance) are designed to be temporary — Amodei himself wrote that autonomous weapons "may prove critical" once the technology matures. The broader significance: every major AI corporation is positioning itself to serve US imperialism; the only question is which one gets the contracts first.
Sources WSWS Trump blacklists Anthropic
### "Mamdani's decision to reopen New York City schools after blizzard angers teachers and parents" — Sandy English English's article documents the ground-level impact of Mamdani's school decision (covered in Top Stories above). The article's unique contribution is its focus on the UFT's role: the union put up "no opposition" to the reopening despite dangerous conditions — teachers and parents were left to organize opposition through social media and an online petition with no institutional support. English connects this to the broader pattern of union bureaucratic complicity with the political establishment, regardless of which party or tendency holds office. The school decision was not incidental — it expressed the same class allegiance that drove Mamdani to the Oval Office two days later.
Sources WSWS Mamdani's decision to reopen NYC schools after blizzard angers teachers and parents
### "Argentina's labor bill vote and tire factory shutdown expose pseudo-left unionism" — Andrea Lobo Lobo's article provides analysis beyond the facts covered above: the CGT's conduct during the Senate vote echoes its impotence during the lower house vote. --- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Jacobin / DSA](#jacobin--dsa) — openly apologizing for Mamdani's collaboration with Trump; framing the DSA's fundamental class treachery as "savvy" tactical maneuvering - [Left Voice](#left-voice) — opposes the Iran war but channels opposition into a "movement" framework with no socialist program or critique of reformist misleadership - [Socialist Alternative](#socialist-alternative) — calls for a nationwide general strike on May 1, but framed as a pressure tactic within the union bureaucracy rather than a revolutionary instrument - [SWP (UK)](#swp-uk) — headline opposition to the Iran attack alongside celebration of the Green Party's by-election win as a progressive breakthrough - [Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT)](#socialist-appeal--rcp-imt) — correct on Labour's crisis but no Iran war coverage visible; positioning the "Communist Party" brand as an electoral alternative rather than building genuine revolutionary perspective ### Jacobin / DSA The day's most politically significant Jacobin piece is Meagan Day's **"Zohran Mamdani Is Trying a Fresh Tactic With Trump"** — an open apologia for the DSA mayor's second secret meeting with Trump. Day characterizes the meeting as "conciliatory" and describes it as "both a savvy and potentially perilous strategy," treating collaboration with fascism as a matter of tactical risk rather than class betrayal. The framing converges directly with the bourgeois press's "Is Mamdani playing 4D chess?" narrative, providing a left cover for what the WSWS Perspective correctly identified as an act of political treachery comparable to Pierre Laval's collaboration with the Nazis. Jacobin also published **"Spain's Radical Left Is in Trouble but Not Defeated Yet"**, covering Labor Minister Yolanda Diaz (Sumar) stepping down and calling for a "united left-wing front" for Spain's next general election. This is standard national-reformist fare: electoral coalitions presented as the path forward for the working class, with no discussion of independent class politics or the bankruptcy of the Podemos/Sumar experiment in managing Spanish capitalism. A third piece, **"The Genocide in Gaza Has Not Ended"**, by Jerusalem-based historian Lee Mordechai, correctly argues that genocide continued after the Trump-brokered ceasefire. The article is factually valuable but stops at expose without class analysis, anti-imperialist conclusions, or any call for independent working-class mobilization. On the day the US launched a war on Iran — a direct extension of the same imperialist aggression devastating Gaza — the absence of any connection to the broader war drive is politically significant. The overall Jacobin/DSA orientation for the day: open apologetics for collaboration with Trumpism (Mamdani), reformist electoral coalitionism (Spain), and factual criticism of Israel that studiously avoids anti-imperialist conclusions (Gaza). The class interests expressed: the upper-middle-class layer that supports the Democratic Party and fears independent working-class action. ### Left Voice Left Voice published **"No War on Iran: We Must Build an Anti-Imperialist Movement to Stop Trump"** — opposing the US-Israeli attack and calling for "the strongest repudiation in the streets by the working class and young people." The article correctly characterizes the war as imperialist and provides useful factual details (operation "Lion's Roar," USS Lincoln as launch platform). However, the anti-war sentiment is framed within a "movement" perspective that avoids an explicit socialist program, offers no critique of the DSA and reformist misleadership of the anti-war movement, and is likely to channel opposition into DSA-friendly coalition politics rather than independent working-class organization. A second piece, **"UAW Local 2325 Commits to a Strike Vote to Fight ICE"**, covers UAW Local 2325 (legal services workers) committing to a strike vote. Left Voice promotes the action without critically analyzing the UAW bureaucracy's systemic role in containing and betraying past strike waves — treating bureaucratic strike talk as genuine working-class resistance. The overall Left Voice orientation: anti-imperialist posture without revolutionary program; adaptation to union bureaucracy without class critique. ### Socialist Alternative Socialist Alternative published **"Trump Netanyahu's War On Iran"**, an ISA international editorial opposing the US-Israeli attack and calling for an anti-war movement, referencing the Minneapolis general strike. The site also features prominently **"The Case for a Nationwide General Strike on May 1"** and **"Students for a General Strike"** (a Yale student's call for a student-worker general strike). The general strike rhetoric is significant but politically hollow. SAlt frames the general strike as a pressure tactic — a demonstration of displeasure designed to force concessions — rather than a revolutionary instrument of working-class power. There is no discussion of the need to break from the union bureaucracy, build rank-and-file committees, or construct an independent party of the working class. The absence of a socialist program behind the "general strike" call transforms it into militant-sounding reformism — the same approach that has repeatedly channeled working-class militancy into dead ends. ### SWP (UK) The SWP's Socialist Worker led with **"United States and Israel launch murderous assault on Iran / Iran has retaliated"** as its top headline — straightforward opposition to the attack. The SWP historically channels anti-war sentiment into bourgeois "Stop the War Coalition" (STWC) frameworks without building independent working-class structures; it remains to be seen whether the Iran war triggers a revival of this approach. Domestically, the SWP published **"Gorton and Denton campaigners: We're 'buzzing' after by-election"**, celebrating the Green Party's by-election victory as a "boost for anti-racists." This is characteristic SWP adaptation to reformist electoral politics — supporting the Green Party, a bourgeois environmental reformist party, as a vehicle for social progress with no class analysis. The SWP also covered its own electoral vehicle, "Your Party," in leadership election results — building yet another reformist party rather than developing independent working-class political perspective. ### Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT) Adam Booth published **"Green Party Labour's Gorton Denton defeat: Another political earthquake"**, welcoming Labour's humiliation but warning that the Greens' "reformism offers no way out of the crisis." This positions the RCP/IMT as the "real left" alternative to both Greens and Labour — correct in its criticism of Green reformism but framed within the IMT's own project of building an electoral "Communist Party" that will participate in parliamentary politics. The critique of Green reformism is undermined by the IMT's parallel pursuit of electoral reformism under a different banner. A second piece, **"Chagos Islands deal blows up in Starmer's face"**, covers Trump blocking the UK's Chagos handover deal, correctly observing British imperialism's subordination to the US. However, it remains framed within a "Starmer fails" narrative without developing an analysis of British capitalism's structural crisis. Notably, no Iran war coverage was visible on the RCP/Socialist Appeal site as of the time of the research agent's fetch — a significant absence on the day of the most important US military operation in over two decades. --- ## What the WSWS should cover today ### 1. International condemnation of US-Israeli attack on Iran: China, Russia, Global South responses The US-Israeli attack on Iran is the most significant international event since the 2003 Iraq invasion. China expressed "deep concern." Russia's response — given its relationship with both Iran and the US over Ukraine — is of enormous strategic significance. Turkey, Saudi Arabia, India, and other Global South responses reveal new fault lines in the decomposition of the post-WWII world order. A comprehensive WSWS analysis of international reactions would illuminate the geopolitical dynamics of the escalating war.
Recommended reading SCMP Israel claims top Iranian officials dead, but Iran says leadership still intact
### 2. US Congress response to Iran strikes: constitutional crisis of unauthorized war Multiple US lawmakers from both parties condemned Trump's Iran strikes as "acts of war unauthorized by Congress." The WSWS should analyze the constitutional dimensions, the War Powers Act being bypassed, and the bipartisan complicity that enabled this — Democrats funded the military apparatus that is now being used.
Recommended reading Guardian US lawmakers condemn Trump over Iran strikes: "acts of war unauthorized by Congress"
### 3. Pakistan-Afghanistan war: update amid regional conflagration Yesterday's top story — Pakistan's "open war" declaration against Afghanistan — risks being buried by the Iran war. But the Pakistan-Afghanistan conflict involves a nuclear-armed state and has destabilization implications touching Iran, India, and China. With the entire region now in upheaval, a WSWS article connecting the Pakistan-Afghanistan and Iran wars as part of a broader imperialist destabilization of Central and South Asia is urgently needed.

### 5. Biden's reemergence and the Democrats' midterm crisis Biden's rare public appearance — warning Trump will "steal" the 2026 midterms while undergoing cancer treatment — signals important developments in the Democratic Party's response to Trumpism. ### 6. Iran war and global aviation: class dimensions of civilian disruption Eight countries shutting airspace, airlines suspending Middle East operations, hundreds of thousands of travelers stranded — the immediate civilian fallout of the attack. journalists attacked with rocks ### 1. Khamenei confirmed dead as US-Israel war on Iran enters Day 2 Iran confirmed the death of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the US-Israeli airstrikes launched on February 28, declaring 40 days of mourning and seven days of public holidays. President Masoud Pezeshkian called the killing "a great crime." A three-person council — Pezeshkian, the chief of the judiciary, and a Guardian Council jurist — has assumed temporary leadership until a new supreme leader is elected. Top security officials, Khamenei's daughter, son-in-law, and grandson were also killed in the strike on his compound. On Day 2, Israel launched fresh waves of strikes on "the heart of Tehran," targeting police headquarters, Iranian state television, the revolutionary court, and a defense ministry building. The death toll from the strike on a girls' elementary school in Minab, Hormozgan province — located 600 metres from an IRGC base — rose to 148 killed and 95 injured, according to Iranian state media authenticated by BBC Verify. The Iranian Red Crescent reports at least 201 killed and 747 hurt across Iran since Saturday. Iran's IRGC claims it has struck 27 bases hosting US troops across the region plus Israeli military facilities. Dubai International Airport — the world's busiest by passenger traffic — was damaged; an Abu Dhabi airport drone strike killed one person and injured seven; Doha airport was hit. A vessel was struck by an unknown projectile 50 nautical miles north of Muscat. Iran's navy reportedly warned tankers the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to shipping. Oman confirmed an oil tanker was attacked in the strait. As David North wrote in the WSWS Perspective published the same day the attack was launched: "No amount of media propaganda can disguise the fact that the attack on Iran is precisely what was described at the Nuremberg trials of Nazi leaders back in 1945-46 as a 'crime against peace.'" North characterized the assault as "a massive political crime, illegal under international law and in direct violation of the US Constitution," carried out "without even the figment of authorization from Congress, against a country which has not attacked the United States and poses no threat to it." ### 4. Trump revokes EPA endangerment finding in "largest deregulation in American history" Trump signed an executive action revoking the 2009 EPA "endangerment finding" — the Obama-era ruling that determined greenhouse gases (CO2, methane, nitrous oxide, and others) constitute a threat to public health. This ruling has served as the legal foundation for virtually all federal climate regulation, including vehicle emission standards. The White House called the revocation "the largest deregulation in American history" and claimed it would reduce per-vehicle costs for automakers by $2,400. Environmental groups described it as "by far the most significant rollback on climate change yet attempted" and said they will challenge it in courts. Trump called the finding "a disastrous Obama era policy" and "the legal foundation for the Green New Scam." In a rare public comment, Barack Obama responded: "Without it, we'll be less safe, less healthy and less able to fight climate change — all so the fossil fuel industry can make even more money." The timing is significant — the action was signed as global attention was focused on the Iran war, effectively burying what is arguably the most consequential single regulatory action on climate in US history. By eliminating the legal basis for all federal emissions standards, it removes the government's capacity to regulate greenhouse gas emissions at the federal level. Key facts: - **White House:** "Largest deregulation in American history"; claims $2,400 per-vehicle savings - **Trump:** Called the finding "a disastrous Obama era policy that severely damaged the American auto industry" - **Obama:** "Less safe, less healthy and less able to fight climate change — all so the fossil fuel industry can make even more money" - **Environmental groups:** Characterized as "by far the most significant rollback on climate change yet attempted"; court challenges expected
Sources BBC Trump revokes landmark EPA ruling that greenhouse gases endanger public health
### 5. Israel bans 37 aid organizations from Gaza and the West Bank Israel gave 37 major humanitarian organizations — including Medecins Sans Frontieres, Oxfam, Save the Children, ActionAid, and the Norwegian Refugee Council — 60 days to comply with new registration rules or cease operations in Gaza and the West Bank. The organizations petitioned Israel's High Court; Justice Dafna Barak-Erez granted a temporary injunction, stating there was "a real legal dispute requiring more time to work through." Four months into the Gaza ceasefire, more than 2 million people depend on these organizations for food, medical care, and water. West Bank Palestinians have been forced off land by settlement expansion. The organizations argue that Israel, as an occupying power, has obligations under international humanitarian law to permit their operations. The organizations warned that banning them would cause "irreparable harm." The timing coincides with global attention focused on the Iran war, raising concerns that the humanitarian crisis in Gaza and the West Bank is being deliberately intensified under cover of the broader regional conflict.
Sources BBC Aid groups in Gaza and West Bank thrown lifeline as Israel court pauses ban threat
### 6. Bolivian military plane carrying banknotes crashes in El Alto, at least 20 killed A Bolivian air force C-130 Hercules crashed at El Alto International Airport near La Paz at 18:15 local time Friday while arriving from Santa Cruz. The aircraft was transporting banknotes to the Central Bank of Bolivia. At least 20 people were killed and 31 injured. --- ## International developments - [Belgium seizes Russian shadow fleet tanker in first-ever physical interception](#belgium-seizes-russian-shadow-fleet-tanker-in-first-ever-physical-interception) — Belgian special forces intercepted the vessel overnight in the North Sea; previous sanctions actions had targeted designations but never physically seized a ship - [Jersey parliament passes assisted dying law](#jersey-parliament-passes-assisted-dying-law) — first legal assisted deaths could occur as early as next summer, marking a significant legislative precedent in the British Isles ### Belgium seizes Russian shadow fleet tanker in first-ever physical interception Belgian special forces intercepted a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker in an overnight North Sea operation and escorted it to the port of Zeebrugge for seizure. Belgian Foreign Minister Maxime Prevot confirmed the operation via social media. Defence Minister Theo Francken praised the "exceptional professionalism and courage" of the special forces. This is the first known physical seizure of a Russian shadow fleet vessel. Earlier actions have targeted sanctions designations but not the vessels themselves. The shadow fleet has been used to evade Western oil sanctions and fund Russia's war in Ukraine. The operation represents a significant escalation of the European sanctions enforcement regime and comes as global attention is consumed by the Iran war.
Sources SCMP Belgium special forces seize Russian shadow fleet tanker
### Jersey parliament passes assisted dying law Jersey's parliament passed an assisted dying law in what marks "the final legislative stage" before Royal Assent. The first legal assisted deaths could happen as early as next summer. The law makes Jersey one of the first jurisdictions in the British Isles to permit assisted dying. The legislative movement for assisted dying across the British Isles raises broader questions about healthcare access, quality of life, and social inequality in declining capitalist states — where access to palliative care remains deeply unequal and the political establishment frames the debate in terms of individual "choice" rather than the systemic failures of public health systems.
Sources BBC Jersey parliament passes assisted dying law
--- ## World economy - [Oil set to surge 9% as Iran war threatens global supply](#oil-set-to-surge-9-as-iran-war-threatens-global-supply) — WTI heading to $73+/barrel; Barclays warns of $80 in "material supply disruption"; Royal Bank of Canada: "$100+ oil is a clear and present danger" - [Gold, silver, and crypto surge on war risk](#gold-silver-and-crypto-surge-on-war-risk) — gold at $5,400/oz (+2.25%); Bitcoin tops $68,000; Ether, Solana, XRP up to 10% - [De-dollarization accelerates](#de-dollarization-accelerates) — trade-weighted dollar lost 7% over past year; USD share of global FX reserves fell from 71% (2001) to 57% (Q4 2025) ### Oil set to surge 9% as Iran war threatens global supply The Iran war has triggered the most significant disruption to global energy markets since the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine. With Iran effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz and multiple tanker owners, oil majors, and trading houses suspending crude and LNG shipments, markets are bracing for a potentially historic Monday open. US crude oil (WTI) is on track to rise more than 9% when trading resumes Monday, from $67 to above $73 per barrel — the highest since June 2025 when Iran's nuclear facilities were struck. Barclays projects oil could reach $80/barrel "in the event of material supply disruption." The Royal Bank of Canada reports that regional leaders warned Washington that "$100+ oil is a clear and present danger." Oman confirmed an oil tanker was attacked in the strait, and the IRGC reportedly warned tankers that no ship would be allowed to pass. OPEC+ held an emergency meeting Sunday and is considering raising output by 206,000 barrels per day in April — above its original 137,000 target — to counter the supply disruption. IEA Director Fatih Birol said the agency is "actively monitoring" the Hormuz situation and is in contact with major producer ministers. Ship insurance costs are surging due to the Iranian missile and drone threat across the Persian Gulf. **Market data (weekend/Sunday trading):** - **WTI crude oil:** $73+/barrel (up 9% from $67 Friday close) — Guardian/IG - **Brent crude:** Expected similar gains - **Gold:** ~$5,400/oz (up 2.25%) — Guardian/IG - **Silver:** Up 3.2% — Guardian/IG - **Bitcoin:** Topped $68,000 after Khamenei death confirmed — CoinDesk - **Ether, Solana, XRP:** Surged up to 10% as crypto recovered Saturday's war-driven losses — CoinDesk - **Polymarket:** Iran strike prediction market among most-traded contracts ever; $1.2 million in reported insider profits on Iran strike bets — CoinDesk - **Saudi market (Sunday):** Down 2.5%; Saudi Aramco shares up 2.5% on oil price spike — Guardian - **FTSE 100:** Expected to fall ~0.5% at Monday open (had been near record 11,000) — Guardian - **US markets:** Closed Sunday; futures expected to open significantly lower Monday - **Asian markets (Nikkei, Shanghai, Hang Seng):** Closed Sunday; will open Monday under Iran war shadow **De-dollarization:** The trade-weighted dollar has lost 7% of its value over the past year. --- ## Science, technology, and public health - [Measles cases reach 1,136 in the US as of Feb 26](#measles-cases-reach-1136-in-the-us-as-of-feb-26) — 28 jurisdictions reporting, 10 new outbreaks in 2026, 90% of cases among unvaccinated individuals; on track to far exceed the worst surge in decades - [Trump's EPA endangerment finding revocation threatens public health framework](#trumps-epa-endangerment-finding-revocation-threatens-public-health-framework) — eliminates federal capacity to regulate greenhouse gases as a public health threat - [First UK baby born from deceased donor womb transplant](#first-uk-baby-born-from-deceased-donor-womb-transplant) — world first opens new possibilities for women with MRKH syndrome, which affects 1 in 5,000 UK women - [Unregulated peptide injections surge as social media drives wellness trend](#unregulated-peptide-injections-surge-as-social-media-drives-wellness-trend) — 8% of products contaminated with bacterial endotoxins; no human trials for most compounds ### Measles cases reach 1,136 in the US as of Feb 26 The CDC reports 1,136 confirmed measles cases in the United States in 2026, reported by 28 jurisdictions, with 10 new outbreaks. Ninety percent of confirmed cases are among unvaccinated individuals or those with unknown vaccination status. Just six cases involve international visitors. The 1,136 figure as of late February already far exceeds pre-COVID annual typical ranges and is on track to significantly exceed the 2024-25 surge — itself the worst in decades. The acceleration of measles in the US reflects the compounding effects of vaccine hesitancy, misinformation, and the ongoing erosion of public health infrastructure.
Sources CDC Measles data and research
### Trump's EPA endangerment finding revocation threatens public health framework Beyond its implications for climate regulation (covered in Top stories), the revocation of the EPA's 2009 endangerment finding has direct public health consequences. The finding determined that six greenhouse gases endanger public health and welfare, establishing the scientific and legal basis for regulating air pollution that contributes to respiratory disease, cardiovascular illness, and premature death. By revoking this finding, the administration eliminates the federal government's capacity to regulate these pollutants as public health threats — a decision that will disproportionately impact working-class communities living near industrial facilities and transportation corridors. ### First UK baby born from deceased donor womb transplant Hugo, now 10 weeks old, was born to Grace Bell — who was born without a viable womb due to MRKH syndrome — via a womb transplanted from a deceased donor. The surgery was performed by teams at Oxford and London; Hugo was born at Queen Charlotte's and Chelsea Hospital in December 2025, weighing nearly 7 pounds. This is a world first — a womb transplant from a deceased donor resulting in a live birth in the UK. MRKH syndrome affects approximately 1 in 5,000 women in the UK. The achievement opens new reproductive possibilities, though questions of equitable access to such advanced procedures remain.
Sources BBC First UK baby born from womb transplant from deceased donor
### Unregulated peptide injections surge as social media drives wellness trend A BBC investigation documents the surging use of unregulated peptides — including GHK-Cu, BPC 157, and TB 500 — sold as "research chemicals not for human use" but self-injected by a growing number of people following social media wellness influencers. Testing found that 8% of products may be contaminated with bacterial endotoxins. No human clinical trials exist for most of these compounds. Experts warn of serious health risks from contamination, unknown drug interactions, and the absence of standardized dosing. The phenomenon reflects the broader commodification of healthcare and the desperation of workers to find affordable wellness solutions outside an increasingly inaccessible healthcare system. It also exposes the failure of regulatory agencies to address the rapidly growing unregulated wellness market.
Sources BBC Wellness peptide craze: why people are injecting drugs 'not for human consumption'

### Neil Sedaka, legendary pop songwriter, dies Neil Sedaka, the legendary singer-songwriter responsible for classic pop hits including "Breaking Up Is Hard to Do," "Bad Blood," "Calendar Girl," "Oh! Carol," "Laughter in the Rain," and "Love Will Keep Us Together," has died. Sedaka was a towering figure of American popular music from the late 1950s through the 1970s, a Brill Building songwriter who helped shape the sound of commercial pop during a period when the American music industry was undergoing a transformative democratization. Sedaka's death marks the continued passing of a generation of American popular musicians who defined mid-twentieth-century mass culture. His songwriting, rooted in Tin Pan Alley craftsmanship but attuned to the emerging youth market of the late 1950s, produced melodies of genuine emotional directness and formal elegance. Whatever the limitations of the commercial pop idiom, Sedaka's best work achieved a quality of accessible artistic achievement that reflected the cultural confidence — and contradictions — of postwar American capitalism at its height.
Sources Variety Neil Sedaka, legendary singer-songwriter, dies
### Producers Guild awards: "One Battle After Another" wins top prize The Producers Guild of America Awards saw "One Battle After Another" win the top prize. The PGA Award is historically the most reliable predictor of the Best Picture winner at the Oscars, making this result a significant bellwether for the upcoming Academy Awards. The award comes as Hollywood navigates a period of profound industry transformation: AI-driven job cuts continue to reshape production workflows, SAG-AFTRA contract negotiations are beginning, and the political atmosphere surrounding the film industry has become increasingly charged — as evidenced by the Sarandon blacklisting and the BAFTA incident.

### Hamnet, Hamlet and the demanding effort "to show the very age and body of the time" — WSWS Staff A review of Chloe Zhao's film *Hamnet* (based on Maggie O'Farrell's 2020 novel), examining Shakespeare's family life and the death of his son as the inspiration for *Hamlet*. The film has been nominated for numerous Academy Awards. The review engages with A.C. Bradley's critical analysis of *Hamlet* and the demands of genuine artistic achievement — the effort "to show the very age and body of the time his form and pressure." The piece argues for an understanding of artistic creation as rooted in lived historical experience rather than abstract genius.

--- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Jacobin / DSA](#jacobin--dsa) — defends Mamdani's collaboration with Trump as "trying a fresh tactic"; attacks the Iran war as Trump's personal failure without class analysis of imperialism - [Left Voice (PTS/FT)](#left-voice-ptsft) — opposes both the Iranian regime and US imperialism but promotes a nationalist-reformist framework for "who decides Iran's future" - [Socialist Alternative (ISA/CWI)](#socialist-alternative-isacwi) — calls for a May 1 "general strike" as an abstract slogan divorced from any political program or party-building perspective - [SWP UK (IST)](#swp-uk-ist) — moralistic anti-war stance without class analysis; articles on Iranian student protests objectively align with regime-change framing - [Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT)](#socialist-appeal--rcp-imt) — focuses on UK by-election as an opportunity for Labour left; continues to promote entry into the Labour Party ### Jacobin / DSA The Iran war dominates Jacobin's output, but the tendency's response reveals its fundamental incapacity to provide a Marxist analysis of imperialism. Branko Marcetic's "Donald Trump, Warmonger-in-Chief" attacks the Iran war as driven by Trump's personal decision "to drag us into war" without coherent strategic logic. While criticizing neoconservatism, the article frames the war as the product of Trump's individual psychology and neoconservative ideology rather than the systemic crisis of US capitalism and the imperialist system as a whole. There is no call for independent working class action, no analysis of the bipartisan character of US imperialism, and no acknowledgment that the Democratic Party voted overwhelmingly for every previous Middle Eastern war. Arron Reza Merat's "There Is No Pretext or Plan for the US-Israel War on Iran" argues the attack has no legitimate pretext, framing it as driven primarily by Israel's agenda. While the article contains useful factual material about the absence of congressional authorization, Jacobin characteristically fails to connect the Iran war to the broader crisis of US imperialism, the internal class war against the American working class, or the need for an international socialist opposition. Most revealing is Meagan Day's defense of Mamdani's second White House meeting: "Zohran Mamdani Is Trying a Fresh Tactic With Trump." Day explicitly defends class collaboration with an authoritarian government, writing: "Governing entails cooperation and compromise, even with a noxious authoritarian president." She characterizes Mamdani as "trying to thread the needle of obtaining concessions from Donald Trump without abandoning his core socialist principles." This is the purest expression of the DSA's political orientation: the defense of collaboration with fascism under the guise of pragmatism. Day implicitly dismisses the principled opposition of the WSWS as impractical "purism." **Overall orientation:** Jacobin's Iran war coverage is moralistic opposition to the war that locates responsibility in Trump's personality and neoconservative ideology, never in the capitalist system. Its defense of Mamdani-Trump collaboration demonstrates that the DSA's real political function is to provide a left cover for the integration of "progressive" politicians into the fascist state apparatus.
Sources Jacobin Donald Trump, Warmonger-in-Chief · Jacobin There Is No Pretext or Plan for the US-Israel War on Iran · Jacobin Zohran Mamdani Is Trying a Fresh Tactic With Trump
### Left Voice (PTS/FT) Left Voice's lead article, "Between Social Unrest and the Threat of Imperialist Military Attack, Who Will Try to Decide Iran's Future?", opposes both the Iranian regime and US imperialism but frames the central question as "who will decide Iran's future." This formulation implicitly promotes a nationalist-reformist program of regime change from within Iran rather than building international working class solidarity against imperialism. It fails to connect the Iran war to the international crisis of capitalism or to call for socialist opposition in the US. A second piece, "Four Lessons from the Fight Against Trump and ICE in Minneapolis," celebrates the reported "retreat" of Operation Metro Surge in Minneapolis as forced by popular mobilization. The article promotes Left Voice's "rank-and-file" strategy within existing union structures, fostering illusions in spontaneous mobilization and union reform without addressing the need for independent working class political organization. No connection is made to the ICFI's analysis of the trade union bureaucracy as an agent of the corporations. **Overall orientation:** Left Voice positions itself as opposing imperialism while providing a nationalist-democratic framework that substitutes for socialist internationalism. Its emphasis on "who decides Iran's future" mirrors the regime-change discourse of the imperialist powers, however unintentionally.
Sources Left Voice Between Social Unrest and the Threat of Imperialist Military Attack, Who Will Try to Decide Iran's Future? · Left Voice Four Lessons from the Fight Against Trump and ICE in Minneapolis
### Socialist Alternative (ISA/CWI) Socialist Alternative's lead piece, "Trump & Netanyahu's War On Iran," condemns the war but substitutes moralistic denunciation for class analysis: "The Middle East is in flames once more, and again it is Trump who has lit the fire. The world's warmonger-in-chief has unleashed a new dangerous situation." The article calls for "protests," "workers' action," and a "general strike on May 1" — all raised as abstract demands divorced from the political fight against both capitalist parties and the need to build a socialist party. A companion piece, "Students for a General Strike," promotes a campus-based campaign for a May 1 general strike, focusing on student activism without connecting it to the broader political crisis or the question of working class political independence. The call for a general strike, when raised outside the framework of building a revolutionary party, serves as a radical-sounding phrase that channels opposition back into the existing political structures. **Overall orientation:** Socialist Alternative's response to the Iran war is characteristic of the tendency — radical-sounding demands ("general strike") raised without any political content that would challenge the domination of the working class by the trade union bureaucracy and the Democratic Party.
Sources Socialist Alternative Trump & Netanyahu's War On Iran · Socialist Alternative Students for a General Strike
### SWP UK (IST) The SWP's Socialist Worker leads with "United States and Israel launch murderous assault on Iran," condemning the attack as "murderous" but locating responsibility primarily in Trump's rhetoric rather than in the imperialist system. The article calls for protests without addressing the need for a revolutionary party or independent working class action. "In a terrifying statement, US president Donald Trump said the 'major combat operations' were launched to eliminate the 'imminent threats' Iran poses." More problematic is "Students in Iran reignite anti-regime protests," which celebrates internal Iranian opposition to the theocracy without distinguishing between popular opposition to the regime and the imperialist regime-change agenda. In the context of an active US-Israeli war on Iran, celebrating "anti-regime protests" without this distinction objectively aligns with imperialist framing. The SWP's long history of adapting to "democratic" imperialism — supporting regime change in Libya and initially in Syria — is relevant context. **Overall orientation:** Moralistic anti-war posture combined with political positions that objectively align with imperialist regime-change operations in Iran.
Sources Socialist Worker United States and Israel launch murderous assault on Iran · Socialist Worker Students in Iran reignite anti-regime protests
### Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT) Socialist Appeal leads with "Labour's Gorton & Denton defeat: Another political earthquake," focusing on the UK by-election result as an opportunity for the Labour left and calling for "seizing the opportunity." The RCP continues to promote illusions in Labour Party reform and the potential for a transformation of the Labour Party from within — the same perspective that has been proven bankrupt by the experience of Corbynism and its defeat. The absence of any significant analysis of the Iran war from the IMT's British section on Day 2 of a major imperialist war is itself revealing. **Overall orientation:** The RCP/IMT remains focused on Labour Party entryism, treating the UK by-election as more significant than the eruption of a major imperialist war.
Sources Socialist Appeal Labour's Gorton & Denton defeat: Another political earthquake
--- ## What the WSWS should cover today ### 1. The WSWS Perspective calls for workers and youth to organize meetings, but there is not yet reporting from the WSWS on actual anti-war protests in the US or internationally, what positions labor unions have taken, or what concrete steps the working class can take. An article documenting international working class opposition to the war — including emergency anti-war meetings, the response of the socialist movement internationally, and the political lessons of past antiwar movements — is urgently needed on the first working day of the war.

Trump revokes EPA endangerment finding: "largest deregulation in American history" threatens public health As detailed in Top stories, this is potentially the most consequential single regulatory action on climate and public health in US history. Unregulated peptide injection crisis: the commodification of wellness The BBC investigation into the surging use of unregulated peptides sold as "research chemicals not for human use" — being self-injected by thousands following wellness influencers — documents a significant public health concern. Iran war Day 3: Hezbollah enters the conflict, RAF Akrotiri struck, three F-15s downed by friendly fire The US-Israeli war on Iran escalated dramatically on Day 3 as the conflict spread beyond Iran's borders into Lebanon, Cyprus, Iraq, and Kuwait. Hezbollah broke the 15-month ceasefire that had been in place since November 2024, launching what it called "a barrage of precision missiles and a swarm of drones" at a military base near Haifa in northern Israel. Hezbollah framed its attack as "revenge for the blood of the Supreme Leader of the Muslims, Ali Khamenei." Israel responded with what IDF Chief Lt. Gen. Eyal Zamir said would be a multi-day "offensive campaign" against Hezbollah targets in Lebanon, striking Beirut's southern suburbs and killing at least 31 people and wounding 149, according to Lebanon's Health Ministry. Iran struck the British RAF Akrotiri base in Cyprus with Shahed-type kamikaze drones overnight, marking the first attack on a British military installation since 1986. Two drones hit the runway; no casualties were reported. The UK is evacuating families and non-essential staff. Home Secretary Yvette Cooper confirmed the strike involved "Iranian kamikaze drones," while insisting Britain is "not at war." The attack followed PM Starmer's statement that UK bases could be used for US "defensive strikes" on Iranian missiles. In a separate incident, three US F-15E Strike Eagles were shot down by Kuwaiti air defense in a friendly fire incident while flying support for Operation Epic Fury during an Iranian missile and drone barrage. All six aircrew ejected safely. The Pentagon separately confirmed three US service members killed and five seriously wounded in an earlier Iranian missile strike on a Kuwait base. Iraq's Iran-backed militias also entered the conflict, firing on a US base in Erbil and claiming a drone attack on Baghdad airport. Trump told the New York Times the operation would last "four to five weeks," adding: "Unfortunately there will be more casualties. That's how it is." Netanyahu said Israel "will strike thousands of targets in the coming days." Iran reported strikes across 24 of its 31 provinces. Iran's ambassador to the IAEA confirmed the Natanz nuclear facility was struck on March 1; a monument to Islamic Republic founder Khomeini was toppled by crowds in southern Iran the same day. Key facts: - **Lebanon:** 31 dead, 149 injured in Beirut (Lebanon Health Ministry); IDF offensive expected to last "several days" - **RAF Akrotiri:** First attack on British military base since 1986; two Shahed drones hit runway; no casualties - **Friendly fire:** Three US F-15Es shot down by Kuwaiti air defense; six aircrew safe - **US casualties:** Three US service members killed, five seriously wounded in separate Iranian missile strike - **Iran:** 24 of 31 provinces struck; Natanz nuclear facility confirmed hit - **Trump timeline:** "Four to five weeks" for the operation (NYT interview)
Sources Al Jazeera Israel kills 31 in Beirut after Hezbollah launches rocket attack · Euronews War widens as Israel, US strike Iran and clash with Hezbollah · Al Jazeera British military base in Cyprus targeted in suspected drone attack · Military Times 3 F-15s shot down by Kuwait in friendly fire incident · New York Times Trump says operation will last "four to five weeks"
### 2. IAEA emergency session: Natanz nuclear strike risks radiological catastrophe The IAEA Board of Governors convened an emergency special session on March 2 after Iran's ambassador Reza Najafi confirmed that Natanz — Iran's primary uranium enrichment site with deep underground centrifuge halls — was struck on March 1. IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi stated the agency currently has "no indication" of structural damage to nuclear installations and radiation levels remain at normal background in neighboring countries. However, Grossi issued an explicit warning: if attacks on nuclear sites continue, a radiological release "cannot be excluded," and the fallout could require evacuations of areas "as large or larger than major cities." Iran's ambassador condemned the strikes as deliberate attacks on facilities under IAEA safeguards, constituting war crimes under international law. The strike on Natanz represents one of the most serious nuclear security crises since Chernobyl in terms of potential risk — the deliberate military targeting of active nuclear facilities under international safeguards is virtually unprecedented. The irony is glaring: Operation Epic Fury was ostensibly launched to eliminate Iran's nuclear "threat," yet the bombing of nuclear facilities creates precisely the radiological catastrophe it claimed to prevent. As the SEP National Committee statement noted, Trump himself had previously declared Iran's nuclear capacity "obliterated" following June 2025 strikes, while Defense Intelligence Agency assessments showed Iran was "years, if not a decade" from intercontinental missile capability. Key facts: - **Natanz struck March 1:** Iran's primary enrichment facility, housing deep underground centrifuge halls - **Radiation levels:** Currently normal in neighboring countries; no confirmed structural damage - **Grossi warning:** Radiological release "cannot be excluded"; evacuations of "major city" scale areas possible - **Iran position:** Strikes on safeguarded facilities constitute war crimes
Sources IAEA Director General's statement to the Board of Governors, 2 March 2026 · IAEA Update on developments in Iran · Tribune India Iran alleges US-Israeli strikes on Natanz nuclear facility
### 3. Global markets crash as Strait of Hormuz effectively closes Global financial markets opened sharply lower on Monday as the expanding Iran war rattled investors and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz threatened global energy supplies. No traceable tanker has transited Hormuz since Saturday night, with 150+ tankers anchored in open Gulf waters. Maersk, MSC, Hapag-Lloyd, and CMA CGM have all suspended Hormuz transits. Between 20% and 30% of global oil and gas supply passes through the strait. Oil prices surged: WTI crude jumped 9% to $73 per barrel from $67; Brent crude climbed 10% to approximately $80 per barrel, the highest since June 2025. Barclays warned of $80/bbl in a "material supply disruption scenario"; RBC called $100+ oil "a clear and present danger." Gold rose 2.25-2.5% to $5,387-$5,400 per ounce. Silver hit $96.93 per ounce on COMEX. Stock markets fell across every region except China. The S&P 500 closed at 6,810, down 1.0% — the worst single-day drop since January. Dow futures fell 500+ points (-1.15%). The DAX suffered the steepest loss in Europe at -2.2%. Defense stocks were the sole bright spot, with BAE Systems up 5%, Leonardo up 4%, Saab up 3%, and Renk up 4%. Saudi Aramco rose 2.5% even as Saudi Arabia's broader index fell 2.5%. ### 4. Congress challenges Iran war powers with bipartisan resolutions Operation Epic Fury was launched without congressional authorization. The White House notified top lawmakers shortly before strikes began but did not seek approval. Bipartisan war powers resolutions have now been introduced in both chambers: Senator Tim Kaine (D-VA) and Senator Rand Paul (R-KY) introduced a Senate resolution requiring explicit congressional authorization before further hostilities; Representatives Thomas Massie (R-KY) and Ro Khanna (D-CA) introduced a parallel House resolution to compel a halt to unauthorized military action. Public opinion is strongly against the war. A University of Maryland poll found only 21% favor the attacks, with 49% opposed. A CBS/YouGov poll found 74% said Trump needed congressional approval. Rep. Jim Himes (D-CT) said: "We have not heard articulated a single good reason for why now is the moment to launch yet another war in the Middle East." However, most Republicans continue to back Trump. Senator Tom Cotton called the operation "ending 47 years of terror"; Senator Ted Cruz called it "the single most important decision of his presidency." Even if the resolutions pass, Trump would veto, and a two-thirds override majority does not exist. The War Powers Act of 1973 was designed precisely to prevent unauthorized military campaigns, yet it has been routinely circumvented by presidents of both parties for decades — a pattern the SEP statement characterized as exposing the "constitutional order's collapse under imperialist war-making." Key facts: - **Senate:** Kaine/Paul resolution requiring congressional authorization - **House:** Massie/Khanna resolution to halt unauthorized hostilities - **Polls:** 21% favor attacks (UMD); 74% say Trump needed approval (CBS/YouGov) - **Veto reality:** Even if passed, Trump would veto; two-thirds majority unavailable
Sources PBS Members of Congress demand swift vote on war powers resolution · NBC Democrats force vote to limit Trump Iran strikes · TIME Iran war: Congress and the War Powers Act
### 5. Iran inside: Khomeini monument toppled as protests collide with US-Israeli bombardment The pre-existing 2025-2026 Iranian protest movement — the largest since the 1979 revolution, sparked by economic collapse in December 2025 — is intersecting with the US-Israeli bombardment in politically contradictory ways. HRANA (the Human Rights Activists' News Agency) estimates 7,015 deaths in the protests as of February 5; the Iranian government acknowledges 3,117. On March 1, a crowd in southern Iran toppled a monument to Khomeini, the Islamic Republic's founder — a symbolically devastating act during the regime's moment of maximum vulnerability. The internal dynamics are complex. Many anti-regime Iranians refuse to celebrate the US strikes, recognizing that imperialist intervention serves Washington's strategic interests, not the Iranian people's democratic aspirations. Meanwhile, the foreign bombardment may generate nationalist rallying around the regime among segments of the population that might otherwise oppose it. Outside Iran, diaspora protests — including celebrations of Khamenei's death in Los Angeles — are politically driven primarily by monarchist and pro-regime-change forces aligned with exiled crown prince Reza Pahlavi, who has intensified his international political activity. Trump reportedly offered three options for new Iranian leadership, signaling the regime-change dimension of the operation that the administration publicly denies. Iranian artists continue to participate in cultural resistance anonymously through encrypted networks. Key facts: - **Protest toll:** HRANA estimates 7,015 killed as of Feb. 5; government says 3,117 - **March 1:** Khomeini monument toppled in southern Iran - **Diaspora:** Celebrations in LA and other cities, driven primarily by monarchist forces - **Reza Pahlavi:** Exiled crown prince increasing international political activity - **Trump:** Reportedly offered three options for new Iranian leadership
Sources Euronews "People of Iran are ready for freedom and democracy" · Britannica 2026 Iranian protests
### 6. Anti-war protests erupt across US and world The ANSWER Coalition designated March 2 a National Day of Action against the Iran war, organizing demonstrations in at least 33 US cities. Key sites included Times Square in New York (hundreds attended), Federal Plaza in Chicago (approximately 100), Portland's Pioneer Courthouse Square (approximately 100), as well as Columbus, Indianapolis, Seattle, and Jacksonville. The PSL organized separate protests in Harrisburg, central Illinois, and dozens of other cities. Internationally, protests ranged from the deadly to the dramatic. In Karachi, Pakistani security forces killed nine protesters on March 1 during demonstrations against the war. In Baghdad, crowds attempted to storm the US Embassy. Protests were held in Srinagar, London, and European capitals. In Los Angeles, a separate pro-regime-change rally of the Iranian diaspora also took place, politically distinct from the anti-war movement. The Trump administration warned of "aggressive enforcement" against protest organizers, adding a threat to democratic rights atop the war itself. Worker opposition documented by the WSWS found only 27% approval for the attack — historically low compared to prior US wars at their outset. Key facts: - **US:** 33+ cities, National Day of Action (ANSWER Coalition) - **Karachi:** 9 killed by Pakistani security forces (March 1) - **Baghdad:** Attempted US Embassy storming - **Approval:** Only 27% support the attack — historically low for a US war's opening days - **Trump threat:** "Aggressive enforcement" warned against protest organizers
