--- ## International developments - [E3 leaders warn Iran of military response as UK drawn into conflict](#e3-leaders-uk-france-germany-warn-iran-of-military-response) — joint statement declares "we will take steps to defend our interests"; Merz says "the days of the Iranian regime are numbered"; UK insists it is "not at war" despite RAF Akrotiri being struck - [Ukraine peace talks scheduled for March 4-5 in Geneva](#ukraine-peace-talks-scheduled-for-march-4-5-in-geneva) — Zelensky endorsed the Iran strikes, calling Iran "an accomplice of Putin"; core territorial and NATO issues remain unresolved after three prior rounds - [Global government reactions to Iran war](#global-government-reactions-to-iran-war) — Japan backs the war while depending on the Middle East for 95% of its oil; Australia endorsed strikes within 3 hours; China calls for immediate ceasefire; Russia protests but analysts say neither can offer Iran meaningful support ### E3 leaders (UK, France, Germany) warn Iran of military response Britain, France, and Germany issued a joint E3 statement on March 1 warning Iran that "indiscriminate and disproportionate" missile attacks would be met with military defense: "We will take steps to defend our interests and those of our allies in the region." The statement effectively aligned the three largest European NATO powers behind the US-Israeli operation without formally joining it. Germany's new Chancellor Friedrich Merz went further, declaring "the days of the Iranian regime are numbered" — the most bellicose statement from a German leader on the conflict. Merz explicitly endorsed the "illegal military assault" and said Germany would not "lecture" allies, despite acknowledging that international law "has relatively little effect." The WSWS characterized this as a qualitative shift in German foreign policy, connecting the Iran war, Gaza, and Ukraine as expressions of imperialist powers' strategy to maintain dominance through force. Starmer maintained the fiction that Britain is "not at war," even after Iranian drones struck RAF Akrotiri. The UK position — allowing US offensive operations from British bases while claiming non-belligerent status — mirrors the legal gymnastics used during prior US wars.
Sources UK Government Joint E3 leaders' statement on Iran · UK Government PM statement on Iran · WSWS German chancellor Merz backs US-Israeli war of aggression against Iran
### Ukraine peace talks scheduled for March 4-5 in Geneva Further US-Ukraine-Russia trilateral peace talks are scheduled for approximately March 4-5 in Geneva. Three prior rounds — in the UAE and Geneva — have failed to achieve a breakthrough. Russia insists on territorial concessions covering the Donbas and rules out NATO troops on Ukrainian soil. Negotiators are reportedly close to a full draft on ceasefire monitoring mechanisms, but core political issues remain unresolved. The Iran war is complicating the Ukraine diplomatic track. Zelensky endorsed the US-Israeli strikes on Iran, calling Iran "an accomplice of Putin" — a statement aimed at tying Ukrainian interests to the broader US military campaign. Russia and China both issued protests against the Iran strikes, but analysts assess neither can offer meaningful military support to Tehran. China called for an immediate ceasefire. Bloomberg reported that Russia is "willing to strengthen coordination" with China through UN and SCO platforms.
Sources Al Jazeera Russia-Ukraine talks: all the mediation efforts and where they stand · NBC Land focus of new Geneva peace talks
### Global government reactions to Iran war Japan's PM Takaichi backed the war with "carefully crafted indirect language" while privately acknowledging — per US DIA and IAEA assessments — that Iran was not developing nuclear weapons. Japan supplies Patriot missiles to the US and conducts intelligence sharing. The WSWS noted Japan's support partly advances its own remilitarization agenda and counters Chinese economic competition; 95% of Japan's oil comes from the Middle East. Australia's PM Albanese endorsed the strikes within three hours of their launch — ahead of most world leaders — without parliamentary discussion. The Pine Gap surveillance base provided targeting intelligence for the operation. The WSWS argued Australia is being transformed into a frontline state for US war preparations against China. Spain's Socialist Party-Sumar government issued a condemnation of the strikes while Spain's military facilities continue to be available for NATO operations. Turkey called for negotiations while maintaining its own complex relationships with Iran and NATO. The pattern across all governments: verbal positioning that masks material complicity.

--- ## World economy - [Oil and shipping crisis](#oil-surge-and-strait-of-hormuz-closure) — WTI crude at $73/bbl (+9%), Brent at $80/bbl (+10%); no tanker transits through Hormuz since Saturday; RBC warns $100+ oil is "a clear and present danger" - [Global equity selloff](#global-equity-selloff) — DAX led European losses at -2.2%; Hang Seng fell 2.14%; defense stocks were the sole winners with BAE +5% and Leonardo +4% - [Crypto and commodities](#crypto-and-safe-havens) — Bitcoin on track for 5th consecutive monthly loss; gold at $5,400/oz; silver near $97/oz - [Corporate developments](#corporate-developments) — Dollar's share of global FX reserves has fallen from 71% (2001) to 57% (end of 2025); war accelerating de-dollarization ### Oil surge and Strait of Hormuz closure The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz is the most consequential economic development of the Iran war's first 72 hours. No traceable tanker has transited the strait since Saturday night. Over 150 tankers are anchored in open Gulf waters. --- ## Science, technology, and public health - [Natanz nuclear strike and radiological risk](#natanz-nuclear-strike-and-radiological-risk) — uranium hexafluoride in damaged centrifuge halls would produce hydrofluoric acid and uranyl fluoride; IAEA inspector access itself is threatened by active bombardment - [Measles outbreak surpasses 1,136 US cases](#us-measles-outbreak-surpasses-1136-cases) — South Carolina approaches 1,000 cases concentrated in Spartanburg County; 93% of patients unvaccinated; CNN warns "expect more deaths" - [COVID-19 and respiratory illness season](#covid-19-and-respiratory-illness-update) — CDC estimates 22 million flu illnesses, 12,000 deaths this season; all three respiratory viruses "quite high and increasing" in January wastewater data - [AI-driven mass layoffs accelerate](#ai-driven-mass-layoffs-accelerate) — US companies announced 108,435 layoffs in January 2026 alone, up 118% year-over-year; Dorsey predicts "most companies will do the same in the next year" ### Natanz nuclear strike and radiological risk As detailed in Top Stories above, the Natanz strike confirmed by Iran's IAEA ambassador triggered an emergency Board of Governors session on March 2. From a technical standpoint, the risks are severe. Natanz's underground centrifuge halls contain cascades of spinning centrifuges enriching uranium hexafluoride (UF6) — a compound that, if released by structural damage, reacts with moisture to produce hydrofluoric acid and uranyl fluoride, both acutely toxic. A breach of containment would not produce a nuclear explosion but could create a chemical-radiological contamination zone requiring mass evacuation. The IAEA's monitoring capacity is itself under threat: inspector access depends on physical presence at facilities now under active bombardment. If IAEA personnel are evacuated — a real possibility — the international community loses its only independent verification of what is happening inside Iran's nuclear infrastructure. The precedent is dangerous: no state has deliberately targeted active, safeguarded nuclear facilities on this scale since Israel's 1981 strike on Iraq's Osirak reactor, which was still under construction and contained no enriched material. ### US measles outbreak surpasses 1,136 cases As of February 26, the CDC has confirmed 1,136 measles cases across 28 US jurisdictions in 2026 — the worst outbreak in decades. South Carolina accounts for approximately 985 cases, with 920+ concentrated in Spartanburg County. Ninety-three percent of patients were unvaccinated. Ninety percent are children, including 26% who are preschool-age. Ten new outbreaks have been declared in 2026. Complications include pneumonia and encephalitis, which can cause deafness and intellectual disability. CNN reported on February 27 that public health officials expect deaths to follow, warning: "Expect more deaths next." The outbreak is driven by vaccine hesitancy promoted by RFK Jr. and the MAHA (Make America Healthy Again) movement, which has been amplified by the Trump administration's hostility to public health infrastructure. The war on Iran is now diverting governmental and media attention away from this escalating domestic health crisis. ### COVID-19 and respiratory illness update The CDC estimates at least 22 million flu illnesses, 280,000 hospitalizations, and 12,000 deaths this flu season. Influenza activity remains elevated nationally, mostly stable or decreasing except in the Pacific Northwest (Region 10), where it is increasing. No new human H5N1 avian influenza cases have been reported in the most recent weekly update. COVID-19 wastewater monitoring data from Biobot Analytics (week of January 3, 2026) showed all three respiratory viruses — COVID-19, influenza, and RSV — "quite high and increasing across all regions." More current March data has not yet been publicly released. ### AI-driven mass layoffs accelerate Block CEO Jack Dorsey eliminated over 4,000 jobs — 40% of the company's workforce, reducing headcount from 10,000 to approximately 6,000 — explicitly citing AI expansion. eBay cut 800 roles (6% of its workforce). In 2025, over 127,000 US tech workers lost their jobs; 150,000+ globally. US companies announced 108,435 layoffs in January 2026 alone — up 118% year-over-year. Amazon accounts for 52% of 2026 tech cuts so far. Dorsey predicted "most companies will do the same in the next year." JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon warned that automation "might lead to a decrease in staffing requirements over the next five years."
Sources IAEA Director General's statement to the Board of Governors · CIDRAP South Carolina's measles outbreak hits 700 cases · CNN Measles surpasses 1,100 cases · CDC Measles data and research · Axios Block, eBay layoffs signal AI-driven workforce cuts
--- ## Arts and culture - [PGA Awards: Paul Thomas Anderson's "One Battle After Another" wins top prize](#pga-awards-one-battle-after-another-wins-top-prize) — PGA has correctly predicted the Best Picture Oscar winner five consecutive years; 98th Academy Awards air March 15 with Conan O'Brien hosting - [Iranian filmmakers face obliteration as war rages](#iranian-filmmakers-under-bombardment-as-awards-season-proceeds) — covertly shot Iranian film "The Friend's House is Here" won Sundance jury award; its filmmakers' country is now under US-Israeli bombardment - [Frederick Wiseman, chronicler of American institutional life, dies at 96](#frederick-wiseman-chronicler-of-american-institutional-life-dies-at-96) — his 45 observational documentaries examined schools, hospitals, courts, welfare offices, and police departments without narration; his 1967 debut was banned for 25 years - [Art world developments in Asia and Europe](#art-world-developments) — sculpture collapse injures four visitors in New York; MACBA director departs Barcelona for Abu Dhabi biennial amid Gulf war ### PGA Awards: "One Battle After Another" wins top prize Paul Thomas Anderson's "One Battle After Another" won the Daryl F. Zanuck Award — the top feature film prize — at the 37th Producers Guild Awards on Saturday night, effectively locking in its frontrunner status for Best Picture at the 98th Academy Awards on March 15. The PGA has correctly predicted the Best Picture winner for five consecutive years. Ryan Coogler's "Sinners," starring Michael B. Jordan, won the SAG ensemble prize and the Music Guild award, maintaining its position as the primary challenger. Conan O'Brien will host the Oscars. The awards season proceeds in its customary bubble of industry self-congratulation, but the backdrop of US war on Iran complicates Hollywood's usual celebratory mode. Israeli-linked donor controversies and threats of Oscars protests loom over the ceremony. The entertainment industry, whose products shape mass consciousness about war, democracy, and social life, continues its calendar as if the world outside were not on fire. Anderson's film — the title itself invoking an endless succession of conflicts — takes on an unintended resonance in the present moment. ### Iranian filmmakers under bombardment as awards season proceeds "The Friend's House is Here," an Iranian film shot covertly and smuggled out of the country, won a Sundance jury award in February. Set during the 2025 Iran-Israel 12-day war, the film depicts Tehran's underground cultural life — street concerts, avant-garde theater — persisting despite regime crackdowns. Now the filmmakers' country is under sustained US-Israeli bombardment, their fellow citizens facing strikes across 24 of 31 provinces. Iranian artists and cultural workers face a double threat: from their own regime's censorship apparatus and from the US-Israeli bombing campaign. Many participate in cultural resistance anonymously through encrypted networks. The juxtaposition is devastating: a film documenting artistic life under bombardment wins an American award while America bombs the country where it was made. This captures the fundamental contradiction of a cultural establishment that celebrates individual artistic courage while the state it operates within annihilates the conditions that produce such art.
Sources NPR Iranian film wins Sundance jury award
### Frederick Wiseman, chronicler of American institutional life, dies at 96 Frederick Wiseman, whose 45 observational documentaries over six decades constituted the most sustained cinematic examination of American institutional life ever undertaken, died at 96 at his Cambridge, Massachusetts home. Working without narration, formal interviews, or musical scores, Wiseman turned his camera on the institutions that constitute the actual fabric of social life: schools ("High School," 1968), hospitals ("Hospital," 1970; "Near Death," 1989), welfare offices ("Welfare," 1975), courts ("Juvenile Court," 1973), police departments ("Law and Order," 1969), and public libraries ("Ex Libris," 2017). His 1967 debut "Titicut Follies" — exposing conditions at Bridgewater State Hospital for the criminally insane in Massachusetts — was banned from public screening for 25 years, a nearly unprecedented act of government censorship of an American film. He received an Honorary Academy Award in 2016. His death coincides with the destruction of the very public institutions he spent his life documenting — libraries defunded, hospitals privatized, schools stripped of resources — under the regime now launching war abroad.
Sources Variety Frederick Wiseman, legendary documentary filmmaker, dies at 96 · NPR Frederick Wiseman obituary

### This week in history: March 2-8 **Fanglin fireworks explosion (25 years ago, March 6, 2001).** An explosion at a Jiangxi province elementary school in China killed 50 children aged 9-11 and 4 adults who had been assembling fireworks as unpaid sweatshop labor. The tragedy exposed a systemic practice: approximately 93% of Chinese schools operated commercial enterprises to address budget shortfalls caused by the restoration of capitalism. The school's children — some as young as nine — assembled explosive fireworks for a local manufacturer, their labor treated as a subsidy for an educational system hollowed out by market reforms. PM Zhu Rongji initially denied the children were making fireworks, blaming a "deranged man," before evidence became overwhelming. **Vitoria Massacre (50 years ago, March 3, 1976).** Spanish Armed Police fired tear gas and live ammunition on workers peacefully assembling at a church in Vitoria-Gasteiz during the post-Franco transition. Five workers were killed and over 150 wounded. The massacre triggered solidarity strikes involving more than 500,000 workers across Spain — one of the largest strike waves in modern European history. The events demonstrated both the revolutionary potential of the Spanish working class and the role of the Stalinist and social-democratic leaderships in channeling working-class anger into the bourgeois "democratic transition." **Assassination of Iranian Prime Minister (75 years ago, March 7, 1951).** PM Haj Ali Razmara was assassinated by Khalil Tahmassebi, an Islamic fundamentalist, removing the last parliamentary obstacle to oil nationalization. Iran's parliament unanimously voted to nationalize the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company — the act that led directly to the 1953 CIA-backed coup overthrowing Mossadegh. This history is acutely relevant: the current US war on Iran is the latest chapter in 75 years of imperial intervention that began with the attempt to maintain control over Iranian oil. **Germany and the League of Nations (100 years ago, March 2, 1926).** Chancellor Hans Luther demanded a permanent Council seat for Germany, warning of non-membership if competing nations received equal status. German imperialism was reclaiming Great Power status barely eight years after its defeat in World War I — a trajectory that within seven years would lead to the Nazi seizure of power. --- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Jacobin / DSA](#jacobin--dsa) — published analysis of Iran war as "neoconservative continuity" without class content or exposure of Democratic complicity; DSA statement condemned war but offered no program while its most prominent elected official collaborates with Trump - [Socialist Alternative (ISA)](#socialist-alternative-isa) — called for "working-class government" in response to Iran war but without revolutionary program; published identity-politics article on "tradwives" and "socialist feminism" - [SWP (UK)](#swp-uk--socialist-worker) — condemned Iran strikes but failed to expose Starmer Labour's direct participation via RAF bases and intelligence sharing - [IMT / marxist.com](#imt--marxistcom) — Alan Woods called for "unconditional defence of Iran" without permanent revolution analysis, collapsing into defense of a bourgeois theocratic regime - [PSL / Liberation News](#psl--liberation-news) — organized emergency protests nationally but without revolutionary program; no exposure of Democratic Party's role in funding every weapons system used in the attack ### Jacobin / DSA **"There Is No Pretext or Plan for the US-Israel War on Iran"** argues the war was launched immediately after Iran agreed to a nuclear deal, revealing it was never about diplomacy. The analysis frames the war as "neoconservative continuity" — a geopolitical category that avoids class analysis entirely. There is no call for independent working-class action, no mention of the Democratic Party's complicity, and no revolutionary program. The war is treated as a policy error by ideologues, not as an expression of imperialist capitalism. **"Trump's Intervention in Iran Will Be a Disaster"** (podcast with Vivek Chibber and Melissa Naschek) discusses the 1979 Iranian Revolution and current protests through the lens of what makes revolution "possible." The framing treats revolution as an abstract sociological phenomenon rather than a question of revolutionary leadership and program — the characteristic method of academic pseudo-Marxism. **DSA statement: "DSA Stands against Imperialist War and with the Iranian People"** condemns the strikes and calls for "solidarity with the Iranian people" but offers no program for independent political action and no critique of Democratic Party complicity. The statement's toothlessness is underscored by the fact that DSA's most prominent elected official — Mahmoud Mamdani at Columbia — is simultaneously conducting personal diplomacy with Trump, collaborating with the very administration waging the war. The day's output reveals Jacobin/DSA's fundamental orientation: opposition to the war is expressed in terms acceptable to the Democratic Party liberal establishment. No bridge to working-class action is proposed.
Sources Jacobin There is no pretext or plan for the US-Israel war on Iran · Jacobin Trump's intervention in Iran will be a disaster · DSA DSA stands against imperialist war and with the Iranian people
### Socialist Alternative (ISA) **"Trump & Netanyahu's War On Iran"** calls the attack an "imperialist threat requiring working-class opposition" and proposes "a working-class government based on public ownership." The demands sound radical but remain deliberately vague — there is no exposure of Democratic Party complicity in funding every weapons system being used, no analysis of the trade union bureaucracy's role in suppressing working-class opposition, and no revolutionary program connecting anti-war struggle to the fight for power. **"Trump, Tradwives, and 'Traditional Values' — We Need Socialist Feminism"** critiques the rise of "tradwife" ideology and argues socialist feminism is the alternative. This represents Socialist Alternative's characteristic adaptation to identity politics: gender oppression is analyzed outside of class relations, and the solution is framed as a specific form of feminism rather than the revolutionary overthrow of capitalism. **"Students for a General Strike"** advocates coordinated student strike action — raising the call for a general strike without any analysis of the union bureaucracies that would need to be challenged to make it real. The absence of any discussion of rank-and-file organization renders the call decorative rather than strategic. Socialist Alternative's output reflects the ISA's standard method: radical-sounding demands detached from any serious analysis of the obstacles workers face — above all, the trade union bureaucracies and the Democratic Party.

The episode — further analyzed in the [pseudo-left press review](#pseudo-left-press-review) below — illustrates the DSA's pattern of direct collaboration with bourgeois political institutions even as it nominally opposes the war. --- ## Science, technology, and public health - [Nuclear safety crisis at Iranian facilities](#nuclear-safety-crisis-the-technical-dimension) — uranium hexafluoride reacts with moisture to produce hydrofluoric acid; at low concentrations causes severe burns, organ damage, death; Natanz, Bushehr, Tehran Research Reactor all at risk - [US measles cases reach 1,136](#us-measles-cases-reach-1136-through-february--highest-since-1994) — 28 jurisdictions affected; South Carolina accounts for 632 cases; RFK Jr.'s HHS has not implemented mandatory reporting - [AI reshaping the labor market](#ai-reshaping-the-labor-market-structural-implications) — executives shifting from describing AI as productivity tool to labor replacement at scale; convergence with war-driven inflation especially dangerous ### Nuclear safety crisis: the technical dimension Beyond the diplomatic implications covered [above](#2-iaea-warns-of-chernobyl-scale-evacuations-as-iran-nuclear-regulatory-goes-silent), the nuclear safety crisis has a technical dimension that warrants closer attention. Iran operates multiple nuclear facilities that process or store hazardous materials: the Natanz enrichment plant (confirmed struck by Iran's IAEA ambassador), the Bushehr nuclear power plant on the Persian Gulf coast, the Tehran Research Reactor in the capital, and several uranium fuel storage sites. The critical substance at risk is uranium hexafluoride (UF6), used in the enrichment process. If released by an explosion or fire, UF6 reacts with moisture in the air to produce hydrofluoric acid — an extremely corrosive and toxic compound that can cause severe burns, organ damage, and death at relatively low concentrations. A significant release at Natanz would require evacuation of surrounding population centers. At Bushehr, a reactor breach would release fission products with contamination patterns depending on wind and weather — analogous to the scenarios modeled after Fukushima. The silence from Iran's nuclear regulatory authority is itself a major concern. It could indicate communications infrastructure damage, personnel casualties, deliberate information suppression, or some combination. Without independent monitoring, the international community is effectively blind to conditions at these facilities. ### US measles cases reach 1,136 through February — highest since 1994 The CDC reported 1,136 confirmed measles cases in the United States through February 26, 2026 — already half the total for all of 2025 (2,281 cases) and the highest year-to-date figure since 1994. Cases have been reported across 28 jurisdictions. South Carolina remains the epicenter with 632 confirmed cases, followed by Utah (117), Florida (64), Arizona (36), and Washington state (24). Ninety percent of confirmed cases (1,023 of 1,136) are outbreak-associated, concentrated in under-vaccinated communities. Robert F. Kennedy Jr., now HHS Secretary, has not implemented mandatory reporting requirements despite the escalating outbreak. No measles deaths have been reported in 2026, but the trajectory suggests the US may exceed 2025's full-year total before summer.
Sources CDC Measles cases and outbreaks · CIDRAP 2026 US measles total nears 1,000
### AI reshaping the labor market: structural implications The wave of AI-driven layoffs documented in the [US section](#ai-driven-mass-layoffs-accelerate-across-tech-sector) has broader structural implications. --- ## Arts and culture - [Golestan Palace damaged in Tehran strikes](#golestan-palace-damaged-unesco-sounds-alarm-on-cultural-heritage-in-middle-east) — Tehran's only UNESCO World Heritage Site hit by debris and shockwave; windows, doors, and mirrors destroyed; Iran pledges formal report to UNESCO - [Middle East museums activate emergency protocols](#middle-east-museums-mobilize-for-war) — Louvre Abu Dhabi on Saadiyat Island threatened by Iranian retaliatory strikes; institutions across the region racing to protect collections - [Iranian artists under siege](#iranian-artists-under-siege-between-regime-repression-and-imperialist-bombardment) — 50+ filmmakers and artists transferred to detention sites near military bases; others communicating through encrypted networks - [Whitney Biennial opens March 8](#whitney-biennial-2026-opens-amid-war) — 56 artists and collectives; geopolitically engaged themes take on new urgency as war unfolds ### Golestan Palace damaged: UNESCO sounds alarm on cultural heritage in Middle East Tehran's Golestan Palace, the city's only UNESCO World Heritage Site and a masterpiece of Qajar-era architecture spanning the 16th through 19th centuries, was damaged on March 1 when a US-Israeli airstrike hit the Arag Square area within the site's buffer zone. The palace's windows, doors, and ornate mirrors were destroyed by debris and shockwave. Iran's minister of cultural heritage, Reza Salehi-Amiri, visited the site on March 2 and described the damage as "a clear violation of international rules and cultural commitments," pledging to submit a formal restoration report to UNESCO. UNESCO issued a broader statement expressing concern over the protection of cultural heritage sites across the Middle East. Iran is home to 29 UNESCO World Heritage Sites and more than 800 museums, representing thousands of years of Persian civilization. The destruction recalls the pattern of cultural devastation in previous imperialist wars — the looting of the Iraq National Museum in 2003, the destruction of Palmyra by ISIS, the bombing of Yemen's Old City of Sana'a. The 1954 Hague Convention for the Protection of Cultural Property in the Event of Armed Conflict, ratified by 133 states, explicitly prohibits attacks on cultural heritage. The US ratified it in 2009. The cultural destruction adds another dimension to the war crimes case already being built around the targeting of nuclear facilities and civilian infrastructure.
Sources Euronews UNESCO expresses concern over cultural heritage sites in Middle East · The Art Newspaper US-Israeli strikes damage UNESCO-listed Golestan Palace, Tehran
### Middle East museums mobilize for war ARTnews reported that museums across the Middle East are activating emergency preservation protocols as the war expands. The Louvre Abu Dhabi, located on Saadiyat Island in the UAE, faces potential threat from Iranian retaliatory strikes on Gulf state targets. The museum houses works spanning 12,000 years of human history, including loans from 13 leading French institutions. Museums in Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait are also taking precautionary measures. The class dimensions of this cultural emergency are stark: the wealthy Gulf states invested billions in cultural institutions as soft power and tourism assets, yet these same states are now enabling the military operations that endanger them. The workers who built and maintain these institutions — largely migrant laborers from South Asia — face the most immediate physical danger.
Sources ARTnews Middle East museums brace for war
### Iranian artists under siege between regime repression and imperialist bombardment Iranian artists face a dual threat: ongoing regime repression and imperialist bombardment. The Iranian Independent Filmmakers Association (IIFMA) reported at the Berlin Film Festival in February that over 50 filmmakers and artists have been transferred to detention sites located near strategic infrastructure and military bases, placing them at direct risk of harm from strikes. Other artists are participating in cultural resistance through encrypted networks, circulating anonymous visual work and music. The situation illuminates a recurring pattern in imperialist wars: the creative intellectuals who document and resist the domestic regime become collateral damage of the foreign intervention that claims to liberate them. Iranian cinema — from Abbas Kiarostami to Asghar Farhadi to Jafar Panahi — has been among the world's most artistically significant national cinemas precisely because it grappled honestly with social contradictions the regime sought to suppress.
Sources Deadline Iranian film body IIFMA collates list of dead and detained artists · Mimeta Iran's artists under siege: from 2022 icons to 2026 anonymous resistance
### Whitney Biennial 2026 opens amid war The 82nd Whitney Biennial opens to museum members on March 4 and to the public on March 8, curated by Marcela Guerrero and Drew Sawyer. Fifty-six artists, duos, and collectives participate, with connections to Afghanistan, Chile, Iraq, Japan, the Philippines, Puerto Rico, Vietnam, and other regions shaped by US political and cultural influence. Roughly 60% of participants were born after 1980; about 30% self-identify as queer. The exhibition's thematic focus on "interspecies kinships, familial relations, geopolitical entanglements, technological affinities, shared mythologies, and infrastructural supports" was conceived before the Iran war. Its opening week now coincides with a major US military operation, giving the work's engagement with American power a more immediate resonance. Whether the biennial's largely academic and identity-based framing can rise to the moment of a shooting war remains to be seen.
Sources Whitney Museum Whitney Biennial 2026 · Cititour Whitney Museum of American Art Biennial 2026 opens March 8

### This week in history: March 2-8 **March 2, 1919 — Founding of the Communist International (Comintern).** The First Congress of the Communist International opened in Moscow, attended by 52 delegates from 34 parties and organizations across 22 countries. The founding represented the organizational culmination of Lenin and the Bolsheviks' struggle against the betrayal of the Second International, which had collapsed into national chauvinism at the outbreak of World War I in 1914. The Comintern's founding manifesto, drafted by Trotsky, declared: "We who have assembled in Moscow, Communists, representatives of the revolutionary proletariat of Europe, America and Asia, feel and consider ourselves the followers and fulfillers of the programme whose banner was unfurled 72 years ago." The congress established the organizational framework for world revolution — a framework that would later be destroyed by the Stalinist bureaucracy's nationalist degeneration. **March 5, 1953 — Death of Joseph Stalin.** Stalin's death at age 74 triggered a succession crisis in the Soviet bureaucracy and opened a period of political instability that culminated in Khrushchev's "secret speech" three years later. The ICFI has analyzed Stalin's rule as the political expression of the bureaucratic caste that usurped power from the Soviet working class, betraying the program of world socialist revolution in favor of the nationalist doctrine of "socialism in one country." **March 7, 1965 — "Bloody Sunday" in Selma, Alabama.** Alabama state troopers attacked 600 civil rights marchers attempting to cross the Edmund Pettus Bridge. The televised brutality — troopers on horseback beating unarmed marchers with clubs and firing tear gas — galvanized national opposition and accelerated passage of the Voting Rights Act of 1965. The event demonstrated both the courage of the civil rights movement and the violent resistance of the Southern ruling class to even minimal democratic reform. **March 8, 1917 — International Women's Day triggers February Revolution.** Women textile workers in Petrograd walked off the job on International Women's Day, demanding bread and an end to the war. Within five days, the strike had spread to 200,000 workers and the tsarist autocracy — which had ruled Russia for over 300 years — was overthrown. The February Revolution confirmed the Marxist understanding that wars create revolutionary crises, as the mass suffering inflicted by World War I found expression in working-class action that no amount of repression could contain.
Sources WSWS This week in history: March 2-8
--- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Jacobin / DSA](#jacobin--dsa) — focuses on war powers constitutionality and public opinion; frames opposition through Democratic Party congressional mechanisms; DSA Councilmember Mamdani collaborated directly with the Trump administration - [Left Voice](#left-voice) — published international declaration calling for "defeat" of US and Israel; militant rhetoric but channels opposition through street protest and union bureaucracies rather than independent revolutionary party - [PSL / Liberation News](#psl--liberation-news) — organizes ANSWER Coalition protests in 33+ cities; characterizes war as "war for empire" but proposes no program beyond street demonstration and congressional pressure - [Socialist Alternative / CWI](#socialist-alternative--cwi) — calls for working-class mass struggle and Iranian workers' revolution; avoids confrontation with union bureaucracies and does not call for a revolutionary party in opposition to pseudo-left organizations - [SWP UK](#swp-uk) — condemns US-Israeli "murderous assault"; criticizes Starmer for enabling strikes from British bases; orients toward building "mass movement" within reformist framework - [RCI / IMT](#rci--imt-socialist-appeal) — Alan Woods publishes "where do communists stand" piece; central slogan "Hands Off Iran — Down with western imperialism"; avoids independent working-class program or sharp break with trade union bureaucracies ### Jacobin / DSA Jacobin's Iran war coverage is dominated by two analytical frames: the war's constitutionality and its unpopularity. Ben Burgis's *Trump's War on Iran Makes a Mockery of American Democracy* argues that only 21% support the war and Trump launched it without congressional approval, but proposes no solution beyond implied congressional action — orienting opposition entirely through the Democratic Party's institutional channels. A second piece, *The Supreme Court May Legalize Donald Trump's War on Iran*, warns that conservative justices could use the war to strike down the War Powers Resolution, expanding presidential war-making power. The article identifies real dangers but its framework assumes the Constitution and courts are the terrain on which wars are stopped — a fundamentally reformist orientation. The DSA's most notable action was Councilmember Shahid Mamdani's meeting with the Trump administration to secure a student's release. This direct collaboration with a government waging an unauthorized war — while the DSA nominally opposes that war — illustrates the organization's thoroughgoing integration into bourgeois politics. The DSA participated in anti-war protests but its political perspective remains bound to pressuring Democratic legislators.
Sources Jacobin Trump's war on Iran makes a mockery of American democracy · Jacobin The Supreme Court may legalize Donald Trump's war on Iran
### Left Voice Left Voice published an international declaration on March 2 titled *For the Defeat of the United States and Israel!* which characterizes the war as "imperialist aggression" and calls for the "strongest repudiation in the streets by the working class and young people." The declaration demands trade unions take action to block war logistics — a formally correct demand — but Left Voice's Trotskyist Fraction (FT) has no perspective for actually challenging the trade union bureaucracies that would need to be overthrown to implement such action. The declaration's militant anti-imperialist rhetoric coexists with an organizational practice of adapting to existing protest movements and their petty-bourgeois leaderships rather than fighting for an independent revolutionary party of the working class. Left Voice's broader Iran coverage frames the conflict as an opportunity to build "the anti-war movement" without specifying the class character or political program such a movement requires, defaulting to the protest-movement politics that leave the fundamental questions of state power and capitalist war-making unaddressed.
Sources Left Voice International declaration: For the defeat of the United States and Israel!
### PSL / Liberation News The PSL's statement *Stop Trump's War on Iran* anchors the ANSWER Coalition's national day of action in 33+ US cities. The statement correctly identifies the war as illegal under both the US Constitution and international law and characterizes it as "a war for empire." However, the PSL's program amounts to street protest plus congressional pressure: demanding Congress "halt this illegal war of aggression." There is no call to break with the Democratic Party, no program for independent working-class political action, and no analysis of the capitalist system as the root cause of imperialist war. The PSL channels genuine anti-war sentiment into a protest-movement framework that poses no threat to the political establishment.
Sources Liberation News PSL statement: Stop Trump's war on Iran!
### Socialist Alternative / CWI Socialist Alternative's *Trump & Netanyahu's War On Iran* calls for "the working class must step in and build mass struggle to end the bloodshed" and states "the revolutionary overthrow of the dictatorship is the task of the Iranian multi-ethnic working class." The CWI's international site (socialistworld.net) published a companion piece arguing for "a workers' government prepared to fight a revolutionary war if necessary, while at the same time starting a socialist transformation within Iran." The rhetoric is more left than Jacobin or the PSL, but the CWI's characteristic evasion is present: it calls for working-class action without confronting the trade union bureaucracies that prevent such action, and it does not call for building a revolutionary party in explicit opposition to the pseudo-left organizations that politically disarm the working class. The CWI's approach is to adapt to protest movements while using socialist vocabulary.
Sources Socialist Alternative Trump & Netanyahu's war on Iran · Socialist World Another war on Iran — Trump moves to consolidate US power
### SWP UK The SWP's *US-Israeli Aggression in Iran Plunges the Region Into Chaos* characterizes the strikes as a "murderous assault" driven by "imperialist ambitions." The article criticizes Starmer's Labour government for allowing British bases in Cyprus to be used for strikes and for British warplanes participating despite claims of non-involvement. The SWP argues "it is up to Iranians to determine their future, not Western bombs." Branch meetings are being organized across Britain under the title "Terror in Iran: Resisting imperialist war in the Middle East." The SWP's orientation remains building a "mass movement" that pressures the Labour government — fundamentally a reformist perspective that assumes the existing state and parliamentary framework can be made to serve anti-war ends. There is no call for a revolutionary break with Labour or for independent working-class political organization outside the trade union and Labour structures.
Sources Socialist Worker US-Israeli aggression in Iran plunges the region into chaos
### RCI / IMT (Socialist Appeal) Alan Woods published *The War on Iran: Where Do Communists Stand?* on communist.red (March 2). The article correctly identifies US imperialism as the primary aggressor and condemns the humanitarian pretext. However, Woods's treatment of the Iranian regime is characteristic of the IMT's method: he acknowledges its "crimes" but immediately relativizes them, writing they "shrink into insignificance" compared to US aggression. This formulation evades the independent political task of opposing both imperialism and the theocratic regime from the standpoint of the working class. The article's central slogan — "Hands Off Iran — Down with western imperialism" — is entirely defensive. There are no demands for independent working-class action, no program for Iranian workers, no call to break with trade union bureaucracies or bourgeois parties. The IMT's "revolutionary communist" branding coexists with a political perspective that does not go beyond left anti-imperialism — the very orientation that historically has led to support for bourgeois-nationalist regimes in the name of opposing imperialism.
Sources The Communist (RCI) The war on Iran: where do communists stand?

Most significantly, Noem labeled two US citizens killed by federal immigration officers — Alex Pretti (37, shot by CBP in Minneapolis in January 2026) and Renee Macklin Good (killed by ICE) — as "domestic terrorists" and refused to apologize. As Jacob Crosse and Anthony Bertolt wrote on the WSWS, the hearing revealed that "behind the theater of bipartisan criticism of Noem's leadership style, both parties support the mass deportation machinery." Key facts: - **Tillis (R-NC):** called Noem's leadership "a disaster" - **Two US citizens killed** by federal immigration officers in Minneapolis in January - **Noem** refused to apologize for calling murder victims "domestic terrorists" - Some bipartisan interest in body cameras for immigration officers and warrant requirements - Democrats reaffirmed support for deportation operations while criticizing Noem
Sources NPR Kristi Noem faces scrutiny at Senate Judiciary Committee hearing · CBS News DHS Secretary Noem testifies before Senate committee · WSWS Kristi Noem defends murderous immigration Gestapo as Senate hearing exposes bipartisan support for ICE

--- ## Science, technology, and public health - [US measles cases surpass 1,136 in 2026](#us-measles-cases-surpass-1136-in-2026) — already nearing half the 2025 full-year total of 2,281 in just two months; only 10 states maintain herd immunity threshold - [H5N1 bird flu pandemic warnings intensify](#h5n1-bird-flu-pandemic-warnings-intensify) — 180 million+ poultry infected in US; 70 human cases; one death; marine mammal spillover confirmed at California seal colony ### US measles cases surpass 1,136 in 2026 The US has surpassed 1,136 confirmed measles cases in 2026, already nearing half the full-year 2025 total of 2,281 in just two months — a rate of infection accelerating far faster than in previous years. Ten new outbreaks have been identified in 2026, with 90% of cases (1,023 of 1,136) outbreak-associated across 28 jurisdictions. South Carolina remains the epicenter with 632 cases, followed by Utah (117), Florida (64), Arizona (36), and Washington (24). Critically, only 10 states plus Washington, DC maintain the 95% MMR vaccination coverage necessary for herd immunity. PAHO has issued an epidemiological alert for continued measles transmission across the Americas. The acceleration of measles is directly connected to the Trump administration's defunding of public health infrastructure and RFK Jr.'s anti-vaccine rhetoric at HHS, which has contributed to declining vaccination rates nationwide.
Sources Scientific American US officially surpasses 1,000 cases of measles in 2026 · CIDRAP 2026 US measles total nears 1,000 · Direct Relief US measles outbreaks 2026 update
### H5N1 bird flu pandemic warnings intensify Scientists warn that H5N1 avian influenza is "completely out of control" and could spark a human pandemic in 2026. More than 180 million poultry have been infected in the United States, with over 1,000 dairy farms reporting outbreaks. H5N1 has been confirmed in seals at California's Ano Nuevo State Park, marking alarming marine mammal spillover. Key facts: - **Human cases:** 70 HPAI A(H5N1) cases in the US (41 from dairy cow exposure, 24 from commercial poultry, 2 from backyard poultry, 3 unidentified source) - **One human death** reported - **CDC risk assessment:** low for general public, but "every new infected host is another throw of the genetic dice" - **No sustained human-to-human transmission** yet detected - **Marine mammal spillover:** confirmed in California seal populations The gutting of CDC capacity under the Trump administration's public health cuts has weakened surveillance and response capabilities at precisely the moment when pandemic preparedness is most needed.
Sources CDC Bird flu situation summary · BBC Science Focus Bird flu 2026 analysis
--- ## Arts and culture - [Berlinale censorship crisis deepens](#berlinale-censorship-crisis-deepens-as-global-festival-directors-rally-behind-tuttle) — 32 global film festival directors rally behind embattled Berlinale director Tricia Tuttle; 80+ artists including Bardem and Swinton sign open letter against "Gaza silence" - [NEA defunding continues under Trump](#nea-defunding-continues-as-hundreds-of-grants-cancelled) — 2026 budget proposes full elimination of NEA, NEH, and IMLS; hundreds of arts groups told their grants are cancelled; Challenge America program ended after 20+ years ### Berlinale censorship crisis deepens as global festival directors rally behind Tuttle The crisis at the Berlin International Film Festival has escalated into a major flashpoint in the struggle over artistic freedom and political speech in wartime Europe. Thirty-two global film festival directors — including Cannes' Thierry Fremaux, Sundance's Eugene Hernandez, London's Kate Matheson, and Toronto's Cameron Bailey — published a statement supporting Berlinale director Tricia Tuttle amid reports she may be fired for failing to prevent political speech at the festival. The controversy erupted when Palestinian director Abdallah Al-Khatib, whose film won the top prize in the Perspectives section, stated during his acceptance speech that the German government is "being partners in the genocide in Gaza by Israel." German Culture Commissioner Wolfram Weimer called the speech "malicious"; CSU politician Hoffmann labeled it "anti-Semitic." The Berlinale's livestream was cut during a subsequent Q&A session — officials claimed a "technical fault" before later posting the full recording. More than 80 prominent artists, including Javier Bardem and Tilda Swinton, signed an open letter criticizing the festival for "Gaza silence." Filmmakers who spoke for Palestinian rights reported being "aggressively reprimanded" by festival programmers. One filmmaker was investigated by police. Jury president Wim Wenders endorsed a "no politics" position. As Stefan Steinberg wrote on the WSWS, the German state's demand that the Berlinale "stay out of politics" while the government actively uses the festival for its own political messaging exposes the hypocrisy of "free expression" claims — the censorship of pro-Palestinian voices mirrors the broader suppression of antiwar speech in Germany. The Berlinale crisis is a concentrated expression of the assault on artistic freedom across Europe and the United States. Art that engages with the most urgent questions of the day — genocide, imperialist war, democratic rights — is being systematically suppressed not through overt censorship but through institutional pressure, professional retaliation, and police intimidation.
Sources Euronews Berlinale crisis: top film festival directors rally to defend Berlinale boss · Variety Javier Bardem, Tilda Swinton sign letter criticizing Berlinale 'Gaza silence' · Hyperallergic How the Berlinale turned into a horror show of German censorship · WSWS German government declares 'no politics' at the Berlinale
### NEA defunding continues as hundreds of grants cancelled The Trump administration's 2026 budget proposes the complete elimination of the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA), the National Endowment for the Humanities (NEH), and the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS). The House Interior Appropriations Subcommittee has proposed a 35% reduction in NEA funding, from $207 million to $135 million. Hundreds of arts organizations across the country have been told their grants are cancelled. The Challenge America program — which has provided funding to small arts organizations serving underserved communities for over 20 years — has been abruptly ended. The pattern mirrors the first Trump administration's proposals in 2017-2018, but this time with substantially more institutional support for full elimination. The defunding of public arts infrastructure is inseparable from the broader political climate in which art that challenges power — whether on questions of war, social inequality, or historical memory — is treated as subversive.
Sources NPR Sweeping cuts hit NEA after Trump administration calls to eliminate the agency · ArtNews National Endowment for the Arts cancels grants

--- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Jacobin / DSA](#jacobin--dsa) — channels Iran war opposition exclusively through congressional procedures and Democratic Party pressure; no call for working-class political independence - [DSA / Socialist Alternative](#dsa--socialist-alternative) — unanimous focus on War Powers Resolution as mechanism for opposing war; explicitly pressures Democrats rather than building independent movement - [German Left Party (Die Linke)](#german-left-party-die-linke) — senior leaders openly endorse the bombing of Iran, with van Aken declaring "May Khamenei rot in hell" — capitulation to imperialist war comparable to SPD's 1914 war credits vote ### Jacobin / DSA Jacobin's coverage of the Iran war continues to frame opposition exclusively through congressional and Democratic Party channels. The most notable article, "Deportation Was Always Political," examines ICE deportation as political persecution centered on the Mahmoud Khalil case at Columbia — but frames the issue entirely within Democratic Party strategy with no class analysis and no mention of the bipartisan support for the deportation machinery documented at the Noem hearing. On the Iran war itself, Jacobin's coverage promotes the congressional authorization framework — the Kaine-Paul War Powers Resolution — as the mechanism for opposing the war. There is no call for international working-class mobilization, no analysis of the war's roots in the crisis of the capitalist system, and no political independence from both parties. The orientation channels antiwar sentiment into precisely the "political trap" identified by the WSWS Editorial Board: procedures that discipline opposition within bourgeois legality while the war continues. The overall orientation is characteristic: Jacobin functions as the intellectual organ of DSA's strategy to pressure the Democratic Party from within, a strategy that has produced no discernible resistance to the escalation from the Democratic Party, which has confined its criticism to management complaints about Noem's style while supporting the deportation apparatus and offering only procedural objections to the war.
Sources Jacobin Deportation Was Always Political
### DSA / Socialist Alternative DSA has issued statements calling for "Congress to exercise its constitutional war powers" — channeling antiwar opposition directly into the Democratic Party's procedural mechanisms. There is no call for political independence from the Democratic Party, no analysis of the bipartisan character of the war drive, and no strategy for building an independent working-class antiwar movement. Socialist Alternative demands a congressional vote and "no war on Iran" — but within the same framework of pressuring Democrats. The organization's politics remain trapped within the orbit of bourgeois parliamentarism: the war is treated as a policy error to be corrected through legislative pressure, not as a systemic expression of imperialism requiring a revolutionary response. The unanimity is revealing. Every major pseudo-left tendency in the US has converged on the same position: congressional authorization as the mechanism for opposing the war. None call for the political independence of the working class. None identify the war's roots in the crisis of capitalism. None propose international coordination of working-class opposition. The convergence reflects their shared class position: organizations of the upper middle class that are organically incapable of breaking with the bourgeois state. ### German Left Party (Die Linke) The most significant pseudo-left development of the day comes from Germany, where Left Party co-leader Heidi Reichinnek and senior leader van Aken have openly endorsed the bombing of Iran. Van Aken declared: "May Khamenei rot in hell" — language indistinguishable from the most bellicose advocates of the war. As Johannes Stern documented on the WSWS, this represents a capitulation to imperialist war by a nominally "left" opposition party in the Bundestag that parallels the SPD's vote for war credits on August 4, 1914. The Left Party, which postures as the voice of social justice and peace in German politics, has collapsed into pro-war chauvinism at the first test. Its leaders are providing left cover for the Merz government's participation in Macron's nuclear escalation and Germany's deepening integration into the imperialist war drive. This exposure should be noted alongside the broader European pattern: the pseudo-left in every country — France (NPA, LFI), Britain (SWP), Germany (Die Linke) — has failed to mount any principled opposition to the war, with tendencies ranging from silence to active endorsement.
